content.content:
1→This is a web application written using the Phoenix web framework. 2→ 3→## Development Environment 4→ 5→This project uses a Nix-based development environment for reproducible Elixir/Phoenix development. 6→ 7→The dev shell should already be loaded via direnv. If not, run `nix develop`. 8→ 9→### Running utilities not in the dev shell 10→ 11→Use `nix shell` to temporarily run utilities: 12→```bash 13→nix shell nixpkgs# -c 14→``` 15→ 16→### Universal Tools Available 17→ 18→- `just` - Task runner (see Justfile if present) 19→- `glow` - Render markdown in terminal (`glow README.md`) 20→- `jq` - JSON processing 21→- `git-spice` - Stacked PR workflow (`gs` commands) 22→- `pre-commit` - Git hooks 23→ 24→### Language-Specific Tools 25→ 26→**Elixir/Phoenix:** 27→- `elixir` - Elixir runtime 28→- `mix` - Build tool and task runner 29→- `iex` - Interactive Elixir shell 30→- `elixir-ls` - Language server for IDE integration 31→- `psql` - PostgreSQL client (version 17) 32→ 33→**Container Tools:** 34→- `docker` - Container runtime 35→- `docker-compose` - Multi-container orchestration 36→ 37→**Note on Language Servers:** 38→Consider using [Lexical](https://github.com/lexical-lsp/lexical) instead of ElixirLS for better performance and features. Lexical can be built from source within this Nix environment. 39→ 40→## Database Setup 41→ 42→PostgreSQL 17 runs in Docker via docker-compose. 43→ 44→**Connection parameters:** 45→- **Host:** localhost 46→- **Port:** 5432 47→- **User:** postgres 48→- **Password:** postgres 49→- **Database:** todo_list_dev 50→ 51→Phoenix database URL format: 52→``` 53→DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/todo_list_dev 54→``` 55→ 56→**Docker commands:** 57→```bash 58→# Start PostgreSQL 59→docker-compose up -d 60→ 61→# Check status 62→docker-compose ps 63→ 64→# View logs 65→docker-compose logs -f postgres 66→ 67→# Stop PostgreSQL 68→docker-compose down 69→ 70→# Stop and remove volumes (DESTRUCTIVE) 71→docker-compose down -v 72→``` 73→ 74→## Common Mix Tasks 75→ 76→```bash 77→# Get dependencies 78→mix deps.get 79→ 80→# Compile project 81→mix compile 82→ 83→# Run tests 84→mix test 85→ 86→# Run tests with coverage 87→mix test --cover 88→ 89→# Database tasks 90→mix ecto.create # Create database 91→mix ecto.migrate # Run migrations 92→mix ecto.rollback # Rollback last migration 93→mix ecto.reset # Drop, create, and migrate 94→ 95→# Code quality 96→mix format # Format code 97→mix credo # Static analysis (if added) 98→mix dialyzer # Type checking (if added) 99→ 100→# Interactive shell 101→iex -S mix # Start IEx with project loaded 102→iex -S mix phx.server # Start server with IEx shell 103→``` 104→ 105→## Git Workflow 106→ 107→This project uses git-spice for stacked PRs: 108→- `gs repo sync` - Sync with remote 109→- `gs stack submit` - Submit all stacked PRs 110→- `gs branch create ` - Create new stacked branch 111→- `gs upstack onto ` - Restack commits onto branch 112→ 113→## Commit Message Guidelines 114→ 115→Use commitizen-style commit messages: 116→ 117→``` 118→(): 119→ 120→ 121→``` 122→ 123→**Types:** feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, perf, test, build, ci, chore, revert 124→ 125→**Examples:** 126→- `feat(users): add user registration endpoint` 127→- `fix(auth): resolve token expiration bug` 128→- `docs(readme): update setup instructions` 129→ 130→Commit messages should explain the **WHY**, not just what changed. 131→ 132→## Troubleshooting 133→ 134→### Dev shell not loading 135→```bash 136→# Check direnv status 137→direnv status 138→ 139→# Force reload 140→direnv allow 141→ 142→# Or manually enter shell 143→nix develop 144→``` 145→ 146→### Mix/Hex issues 147→```bash 148→# Clean Mix cache 149→rm -rf .nix-shell/mix .nix-shell/hex 150→ 151→# Reload shell (will reinstall hex/rebar3) 152→direnv reload 153→ 154→# Or manually reinstall 155→mix local.hex --force 156→mix local.rebar --force 157→``` 158→ 159→### PostgreSQL connection issues 160→```bash 161→# Check if PostgreSQL is running 162→docker-compose ps 163→ 164→# Check PostgreSQL logs 165→docker-compose logs postgres 166→ 167→# Restart PostgreSQL 168→docker-compose restart postgres 169→``` 170→ 171→### Dependency conflicts 172→```bash 173→# Clean all dependencies 174→mix deps.clean --all 175→ 176→# Get fresh dependencies 177→mix deps.get 178→ 179→# Compile everything 180→mix deps.compile 181→``` 182→ 183→## Additional Resources 184→ 185→- [Elixir Documentation](https://elixir-lang.org/docs.html) 186→- [Phoenix Framework](https://www.phoenixframework.org/) 187→- [Phoenix LiveView](https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix_live_view/) 188→- [Ecto Documentation](https://hexdocs.pm/ecto/) 189→- [Elixir Forum](https://elixirforum.com/) 190→ 191→## Project guidelines 192→ 193→- Use `mix precommit` alias when you are done with all changes and fix any pending issues 194→- Use the already included and available `:req` (`Req`) library for HTTP requests, **avoid** `:httpoison`, `:tesla`, and `:httpc`. Req is included by default and is the preferred HTTP client for Phoenix apps 195→ 196→### Phoenix v1.8 guidelines 197→ 198→- **Always** begin your LiveView templates with `` which wraps all inner content 199→- The `MyAppWeb.Layouts` module is aliased in the `my_app_web.ex` file, so you can use it without needing to alias it again 200→- Anytime you run into errors with no `current_scope` assign: 201→ - You failed to follow the Authenticated Routes guidelines, or you failed to pass `current_scope` to `` 202→ - **Always** fix the `current_scope` error by moving your routes to the proper `live_session` and ensure you pass `current_scope` as needed 203→- Phoenix v1.8 moved the `<.flash_group>` component to the `Layouts` module. You are **forbidden** from calling `<.flash_group>` outside of the `layouts.ex` module 204→- Out of the box, `core_components.ex` imports an `<.icon name="hero-x-mark" class="w-5 h-5"/>` component for for hero icons. **Always** use the `<.icon>` component for icons, **never** use `Heroicons` modules or similar 205→- **Always** use the imported `<.input>` component for form inputs from `core_components.ex` when available. `<.input>` is imported and using it will save steps and prevent errors 206→- If you override the default input classes (`<.input class="myclass px-2 py-1 rounded-lg">)`) class with your own values, no default classes are inherited, so your 207→custom classes must fully style the input 208→ 209→### JS and CSS guidelines 210→ 211→- **Use Tailwind CSS classes and custom CSS rules** to create polished, responsive, and visually stunning interfaces. 212→- Tailwindcss v4 **no longer needs a tailwind.config.js** and uses a new import syntax in `app.css`: 213→ 214→ @import "tailwindcss" source(none); 215→ @source "../css"; 216→ @source "../js"; 217→ @source "../../lib/my_app_web"; 218→ 219→- **Always use and maintain this import syntax** in the app.css file for projects generated with `phx.new` 220→- **Never** use `@apply` when writing raw css 221→- **Always** manually write your own tailwind-based components instead of using daisyUI for a unique, world-class design 222→- Out of the box **only the app.js and app.css bundles are supported** 223→ - You cannot reference an external vendor'd script `src` or link `href` in the layouts 224→ - You must import the vendor deps into app.js and app.css to use them 225→ - **Never write inline tags within templates** 226→ 227→### UI/UX & design guidelines 228→ 229→- **Produce world-class UI designs** with a focus on usability, aesthetics, and modern design principles 230→- Implement **subtle micro-interactions** (e.g., button hover effects, and smooth transitions) 231→- Ensure **clean typography, spacing, and layout balance** for a refined, premium look 232→- Focus on **delightful details** like hover effects, loading states, and smooth page transitions 233→ 234→ 235→ 236→ 237→ 238→## Elixir guidelines 239→ 240→- Elixir lists **do not support index based access via the access syntax** 241→ 242→ **Never do this (invalid)**: 243→ 244→ i = 0 245→ mylist = ["blue", "green"] 246→ mylist[i] 247→ 248→ Instead, **always** use `Enum.at`, pattern matching, or `List` for index based list access, ie: 249→ 250→ i = 0 251→ mylist = ["blue", "green"] 252→ Enum.at(mylist, i) 253→ 254→- Elixir variables are immutable, but can be rebound, so for block expressions like `if`, `case`, `cond`, etc 255→ you *must* bind the result of the expression to a variable if you want to use it and you CANNOT rebind the result inside the expression, ie: 256→ 257→ # INVALID: we are rebinding inside the `if` and the result never gets assigned 258→ if connected?(socket) do 259→ socket = assign(socket, :val, val) 260→ end 261→ 262→ # VALID: we rebind the result of the `if` to a new variable 263→ socket = 264→ if connected?(socket) do 265→ assign(socket, :val, val) 266→ end 267→ 268→- **Never** nest multiple modules in the same file as it can cause cyclic dependencies and compilation errors 269→- **Never** use map access syntax (`changeset[:field]`) on structs as they do not implement the Access behaviour by default. For regular structs, you **must** access the fields directly, such as `my_struct.field` or use higher level APIs that are available on the struct if they exist, `Ecto.Changeset.get_field/2` for changesets 270→- Elixir's standard library has everything necessary for date and time manipulation. Familiarize yourself with the common `Time`, `Date`, `DateTime`, and `Calendar` interfaces by accessing their documentation as necessary. **Never** install additional dependencies unless asked or for date/time parsing (which you can use the `date_time_parser` package) 271→- Don't use `String.to_atom/1` on user input (memory leak risk) 272→- Predicate function names should not start with `is_` and should end in a question mark. Names like `is_thing` should be reserved for guards 273→- Elixir's builtin OTP primitives like `DynamicSupervisor` and `Registry`, require names in the child spec, such as `{DynamicSupervisor, name: MyApp.MyDynamicSup}`, then you can use `DynamicSupervisor.start_child(MyApp.MyDynamicSup, child_spec)` 274→- Use `Task.async_stream(collection, callback, options)` for concurrent enumeration with back-pressure. The majority of times you will want to pass `timeout: :infinity` as option 275→ 276→## Mix guidelines 277→ 278→- Read the docs and options before using tasks (by using `mix help task_name`) 279→- To debug test failures, run tests in a specific file with `mix test test/my_test.exs` or run all previously failed tests with `mix test --failed` 280→- `mix deps.clean --all` is **almost never needed**. **Avoid** using it unless you have good reason 281→ 282→## Test guidelines 283→ 284→- **Always use `start_supervised!/1`** to start processes in tests as it guarantees cleanup between tests 285→- **Avoid** `Process.sleep/1` and `Process.alive?/1` in tests 286→ - Instead of sleeping to wait for a process to finish, **always** use `Process.monitor/1` and assert on the DOWN message: 287→ 288→ ref = Process.monitor(pid) 289→ assert_receive {:DOWN, ^ref, :process, ^pid, :normal} 290→ 291→ - Instead of sleeping to synchronize before the next call, **always** use `_ = :sys.get_state/1` to ensure the process has handled prior messages 292→ 293→ 294→ 295→## Phoenix guidelines 296→ 297→- Remember Phoenix router `scope` blocks include an optional alias which is prefixed for all routes within the scope. **Always** be mindful of this when creating routes within a scope to avoid duplicate module prefixes. 298→ 299→- You **never** need to create your own `alias` for route definitions! The `scope` provides the alias, ie: 300→ 301→ scope "/admin", AppWeb.Admin do 302→ pipe_through :browser 303→ 304→ live "/users", UserLive, :index 305→ end 306→ 307→ the UserLive route would point to the `AppWeb.Admin.UserLive` module 308→ 309→- `Phoenix.View` no longer is needed or included with Phoenix, don't use it 310→ 311→ 312→ 313→## Ecto Guidelines 314→ 315→- **Always** preload Ecto associations in queries when they'll be accessed in templates, ie a message that needs to reference the `message.user.email` 316→- Remember `import Ecto.Query` and other supporting modules when you write `seeds.exs` 317→- `Ecto.Schema` fields always use the `:string` type, even for `:text`, columns, ie: `field :name, :string` 318→- `Ecto.Changeset.validate_number/2` **DOES NOT SUPPORT the `:allow_nil` option**. By default, Ecto validations only run if a change for the given field exists and the change value is not nil, so such as option is never needed 319→- You **must** use `Ecto.Changeset.get_field(changeset, :field)` to access changeset fields 320→- Fields which are set programatically, such as `user_id`, must not be listed in `cast` calls or similar for security purposes. Instead they must be explicitly set when creating the struct 321→- **Always** invoke `mix ecto.gen.migration migration_name_using_underscores` when generating migration files, so the correct timestamp and conventions are applied 322→ 323→ 324→ 325→## Phoenix HTML guidelines 326→ 327→- Phoenix templates **always** use `~H` or .html.heex files (known as HEEx), **never** use `~E` 328→- **Always** use the imported `Phoenix.Component.form/1` and `Phoenix.Component.inputs_for/1` function to build forms. **Never** use `Phoenix.HTML.form_for` or `Phoenix.HTML.inputs_for` as they are outdated 329→- When building forms **always** use the already imported `Phoenix.Component.to_form/2` (`assign(socket, form: to_form(...))` and `<.form for={@form} id="msg-form">`), then access those forms in the template via `@form[:field]` 330→- **Always** add unique DOM IDs to key elements (like forms, buttons, etc) when writing templates, these IDs can later be used in tests (`<.form for={@form} id="product-form">`) 331→- For "app wide" template imports, you can import/alias into the `my_app_web.ex`'s `html_helpers` block, so they will be available to all LiveViews, LiveComponent's, and all modules that do `use MyAppWeb, :html` (replace "my_app" by the actual app name) 332→ 333→- Elixir supports `if/else` but **does NOT support `if/else if` or `if/elsif`**. **Never use `else if` or `elseif` in Elixir**, **always** use `cond` or `case` for multiple conditionals. 334→ 335→ **Never do this (invalid)**: 336→ 337→ <%= if condition do %> 338→ ... 339→ <% else if other_condition %> 340→ ... 341→ <% end %> 342→ 343→ Instead **always** do this: 344→ 345→ <%= cond do %> 346→ <% condition -> %> 347→ ... 348→ <% condition2 -> %> 349→ ... 350→ <% true -> %> 351→ ... 352→ <% end %> 353→ 354→- HEEx require special tag annotation if you want to insert literal curly's like `{` or `}`. If you want to show a textual code snippet on the page in a `
` or `` block you *must* annotate the parent tag with `phx-no-curly-interpolation`:
   355→
   356→      
   357→        let obj = {key: "val"}
   358→      
   359→
   360→  Within `phx-no-curly-interpolation` annotated tags, you can use `{` and `}` without escaping them, and dynamic Elixir expressions can still be used with `<%= ... %>` syntax
   361→
   362→- HEEx class attrs support lists, but you must **always** use list `[...]` syntax. You can use the class list syntax to conditionally add classes, **always do this for multiple class values**:
   363→
   364→      Text
   370→
   371→  and **always** wrap `if`'s inside `{...}` expressions with parens, like done above (`if(@other_condition, do: "...", else: "...")`)
   372→
   373→  and **never** do this, since it's invalid (note the missing `[` and `]`):
   374→
   375→       ...
   379→      => Raises compile syntax error on invalid HEEx attr syntax
   380→
   381→- **Never** use `<% Enum.each %>` or non-for comprehensions for generating template content, instead **always** use `<%= for item <- @collection do %>`
   382→- HEEx HTML comments use `<%!-- comment --%>`. **Always** use the HEEx HTML comment syntax for template comments (`<%!-- comment --%>`)
   383→- HEEx allows interpolation via `{...}` and `<%= ... %>`, but the `<%= %>` **only** works within tag bodies. **Always** use the `{...}` syntax for interpolation within tag attributes, and for interpolation of values within tag bodies. **Always** interpolate block constructs (if, cond, case, for) within tag bodies using `<%= ... %>`.
   384→
   385→  **Always** do this:
   386→
   387→      

   388→        {@my_assign}
   389→        <%= if @some_block_condition do %>
   390→          {@another_assign}
   391→        <% end %>
   392→      

   393→
   394→  and **Never** do this – the program will terminate with a syntax error:
   395→
   396→      <%!-- THIS IS INVALID NEVER EVER DO THIS --%>
   397→      

   398→        {if @invalid_block_construct do}
   399→        {end}
   400→      

   401→
   402→
   403→
   404→## Phoenix LiveView guidelines
   405→
   406→- **Never** use the deprecated `live_redirect` and `live_patch` functions, instead **always** use the `<.link navigate={href}>` and  `<.link patch={href}>` in templates, and `push_navigate` and `push_patch` functions LiveViews
   407→- **Avoid LiveComponent's** unless you have a strong, specific need for them
   408→- LiveViews should be named like `AppWeb.WeatherLive`, with a `Live` suffix. When you go to add LiveView routes to the router, the default `:browser` scope is **already aliased** with the `AppWeb` module, so you can just do `live "/weather", WeatherLive`
   409→
   410→### LiveView streams
   411→
   412→- **Always** use LiveView streams for collections for assigning regular lists to avoid memory ballooning and runtime termination with the following operations:
   413→  - basic append of N items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])`
   414→  - resetting stream with new items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], reset: true)` (e.g. for filtering items)
   415→  - prepend to stream - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], at: -1)`
   416→  - deleting items - `stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg)`
   417→
   418→- When using the `stream/3` interfaces in the LiveView, the LiveView template must 1) always set `phx-update="stream"` on the parent element, with a DOM id on the parent element like `id="messages"` and 2) consume the `@streams.stream_name` collection and use the id as the DOM id for each child. For a call like `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])` in the LiveView, the template would be:
   419→
   420→      

   421→        

   422→          {msg.text}
   423→        

   424→      

   425→
   426→- LiveView streams are *not* enumerable, so you cannot use `Enum.filter/2` or `Enum.reject/2` on them. Instead, if you want to filter, prune, or refresh a list of items on the UI, you **must refetch the data and re-stream the entire stream collection, passing reset: true**:
   427→
   428→      def handle_event("filter", %{"filter" => filter}, socket) do
   429→        # re-fetch the messages based on the filter
   430→        messages = list_messages(filter)
   431→
   432→        {:noreply,
   433→         socket
   434→         |> assign(:messages_empty?, messages == [])
   435→         # reset the stream with the new messages
   436→         |> stream(:messages, messages, reset: true)}
   437→      end
   438→
   439→- LiveView streams *do not support counting or empty states*. If you need to display a count, you must track it using a separate assign. For empty states, you can use Tailwind classes:
   440→
   441→      

   442→        
No tasks yet

   443→        

   444→          {task.name}
   445→        

   446→      

   447→
   448→  The above only works if the empty state is the only HTML block alongside the stream for-comprehension.
   449→
   450→- When updating an assign that should change content inside any streamed item(s), you MUST re-stream the items
   451→  along with the updated assign:
   452→
   453→      def handle_event("edit_message", %{"message_id" => message_id}, socket) do
   454→        message = Chat.get_message!(message_id)
   455→        edit_form = to_form(Chat.change_message(message, %{content: message.content}))
   456→
   457→        # re-insert message so @editing_message_id toggle logic takes effect for that stream item
   458→        {:noreply,
   459→         socket
   460→         |> stream_insert(:messages, message)
   461→         |> assign(:editing_message_id, String.to_integer(message_id))
   462→         |> assign(:edit_form, edit_form)}
   463→      end
   464→
   465→  And in the template:
   466→
   467→      

   468→        

   469→          {message.username}
   470→          <%= if @editing_message_id == message.id do %>
   471→            <%!-- Edit mode --%>
   472→            <.form for={@edit_form} id="edit-form-#{message.id}" phx-submit="save_edit">
   473→              ...
   474→            
   475→          <% end %>
   476→        

   477→      

   478→
   479→- **Never** use the deprecated `phx-update="append"` or `phx-update="prepend"` for collections
   480→
   481→### LiveView JavaScript interop
   482→
   483→- Remember anytime you use `phx-hook="MyHook"` and that JS hook manages its own DOM, you **must** also set the `phx-update="ignore"` attribute
   484→- **Always** provide an unique DOM id alongside `phx-hook` otherwise a compiler error will be raised
   485→
   486→LiveView hooks come in two flavors, 1) colocated js hooks for "inline" scripts defined inside HEEx,
   487→and 2) external `phx-hook` annotations where JavaScript object literals are defined and passed to the `LiveSocket` constructor.
   488→
   489→#### Inline colocated js hooks
   490→
   491→**Never** write raw embedded `
   508→
   509→- colocated hooks are automatically integrated into the app.js bundle
   510→- colocated hooks names **MUST ALWAYS** start with a `.` prefix, i.e. `.PhoneNumber`
   511→
   512→#### External phx-hook
   513→
   514→External JS hooks (`
`) must be placed in `assets/js/` and passed to the
   515→LiveSocket constructor:
   516→
   517→    const MyHook = {
   518→      mounted() { ... }
   519→    }
   520→    let liveSocket = new LiveSocket("/live", Socket, {
   521→      hooks: { MyHook }
   522→    });
   523→
   524→#### Pushing events between client and server
   525→
   526→Use LiveView's `push_event/3` when you need to push events/data to the client for a phx-hook to handle.
   527→**Always** return or rebind the socket on `push_event/3` when pushing events:
   528→
   529→    # re-bind socket so we maintain event state to be pushed
   530→    socket = push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})
   531→
   532→    # or return the modified socket directly:
   533→    def handle_event("some_event", _, socket) do
   534→      {:noreply, push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})}
   535→    end
   536→
   537→Pushed events can then be picked up in a JS hook with `this.handleEvent`:
   538→
   539→    mounted() {
   540→      this.handleEvent("my_event", data => console.log("from server:", data));
   541→    }
   542→
   543→Clients can also push an event to the server and receive a reply with `this.pushEvent`:
   544→
   545→    mounted() {
   546→      this.el.addEventListener("click", e => {
   547→        this.pushEvent("my_event", { one: 1 }, reply => console.log("got reply from server:", reply));
   548→      })
   549→    }
   550→
   551→Where the server handled it via:
   552→
   553→    def handle_event("my_event", %{"one" => 1}, socket) do
   554→      {:reply, %{two: 2}, socket}
   555→    end
   556→
   557→### LiveView tests
   558→
   559→- `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` module and `LazyHTML` (included) for making your assertions
   560→- Form tests are driven by `Phoenix.LiveViewTest`'s `render_submit/2` and `render_change/2` functions
   561→- Come up with a step-by-step test plan that splits major test cases into small, isolated files. You may start with simpler tests that verify content exists, gradually add interaction tests
   562→- **Always reference the key element IDs you added in the LiveView templates in your tests** for `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` functions like `element/2`, `has_element/2`, selectors, etc
   563→- **Never** tests again raw HTML, **always** use `element/2`, `has_element/2`, and similar: `assert has_element?(view, "#my-form")`
   564→- Instead of relying on testing text content, which can change, favor testing for the presence of key elements
   565→- Focus on testing outcomes rather than implementation details
   566→- Be aware that `Phoenix.Component` functions like `<.form>` might produce different HTML than expected. Test against the output HTML structure, not your mental model of what you expect it to be
   567→- When facing test failures with element selectors, add debug statements to print the actual HTML, but use `LazyHTML` selectors to limit the output, ie:
   568→
   569→      html = render(view)
   570→      document = LazyHTML.from_fragment(html)
   571→      matches = LazyHTML.filter(document, "your-complex-selector")
   572→      IO.inspect(matches, label: "Matches")
   573→
   574→### Form handling
   575→
   576→#### Creating a form from params
   577→
   578→If you want to create a form based on `handle_event` params:
   579→
   580→    def handle_event("submitted", params, socket) do
   581→      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(params))}
   582→    end
   583→
   584→When you pass a map to `to_form/1`, it assumes said map contains the form params, which are expected to have string keys.
   585→
   586→You can also specify a name to nest the params:
   587→
   588→    def handle_event("submitted", %{"user" => user_params}, socket) do
   589→      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(user_params, as: :user))}
   590→    end
   591→
   592→#### Creating a form from changesets
   593→
   594→When using changesets, the underlying data, form params, and errors are retrieved from it. The `:as` option is automatically computed too. E.g. if you have a user schema:
   595→
   596→    defmodule MyApp.Users.User do
   597→      use Ecto.Schema
   598→      ...
   599→    end
   600→
   601→And then you create a changeset that you pass to `to_form`:
   602→
   603→    %MyApp.Users.User{}
   604→    |> Ecto.Changeset.change()
   605→    |> to_form()
   606→
   607→Once the form is submitted, the params will be available under `%{"user" => user_params}`.
   608→
   609→In the template, the form form assign can be passed to the `<.form>` function component:
   610→
   611→    <.form for={@form} id="todo-form" phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save">
   612→      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
   613→    
   614→
   615→Always give the form an explicit, unique DOM ID, like `id="todo-form"`.
   616→
   617→#### Avoiding form errors
   618→
   619→**Always** use a form assigned via `to_form/2` in the LiveView, and the `<.input>` component in the template. In the template **always access forms this**:
   620→
   621→    <%!-- ALWAYS do this (valid) --%>
   622→    <.form for={@form} id="my-form">
   623→      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
   624→    
   625→
   626→And **never** do this:
   627→
   628→    <%!-- NEVER do this (invalid) --%>
   629→    <.form for={@changeset} id="my-form">
   630→      <.input field={@changeset[:field]} type="text" />
   631→    
   632→
   633→- You are FORBIDDEN from accessing the changeset in the template as it will cause errors
   634→- **Never** use `<.form let={f} ...>` in the template, instead **always use `<.form for={@form} ...>`**, then drive all form references from the form assign as in `@form[:field]`. The UI should **always** be driven by a `to_form/2` assigned in the LiveView module that is derived from a changeset
   635→
   636→
   637→

1→[
     2→  import_deps: [:ecto, :ecto_sql, :phoenix],

content.text:
Here are the complete contents of all eight event model slice files:

## 1. Slice: AddTask
- **Pattern:** Command (State Change)
- **Command:** AddTask with field `description` (text, non-empty)
- **Event:** TaskAdded with fields `task_id`, `description`, `position`
- **Rejection:** Description is empty or blank
- **GWT Scenarios (3):**
  - Add task to empty list
  - Add task to list with existing tasks (new task goes to bottom)
  - Add task when some tasks completed (position based on count)

## 2. Slice: ActiveTaskList
- **Pattern:** View (State View / Read Model)
- **Processes:** All 6 task events (TaskAdded, TaskCompleted, TaskReopened, TaskDescriptionChanged, TaskReprioritized, TaskDeleted)
- **Fields:** tasks list (with task_id, description, position), completed_count
- **Filter:** completed = false AND deleted = false
- **Order:** By position ascending (position 1 = highest priority)
- **GWT Scenarios (8):**
  - TaskAdded adds to empty list
  - TaskAdded adds to populated list
  - TaskCompleted removes and increments count
  - TaskReopened adds to top and decrements count
  - TaskDescriptionChanged updates active task description
  - TaskDescriptionChanged for completed task (no effect)
  - TaskReprioritized moves task to new position
  - TaskDeleted removes active task

## 3. Slice: CompleteTask
- **Pattern:** Command (State Change)
- **Command:** CompleteTask with field `task_id`
- **Event:** TaskCompleted with field `task_id`
- **Rejection:** Task is already completed
- **GWT Scenarios (2):**
  - Complete an active task
  - Reject completing already-completed task

## 4. Slice: CompletedTaskList
- **Pattern:** View (State View / Read Model)
- **Processes:** 5 events (TaskAdded, TaskCompleted, TaskReopened, TaskDescriptionChanged, TaskDeleted)
- **Fields:** tasks list (with task_id, description)
- **Filter:** completed = true AND deleted = false
- **Order:** By completion time descending (most recent first)
- **GWT Scenarios (7):**
  - TaskAdded has no effect
  - TaskCompleted adds task to completed list
  - TaskCompleted adds most recent to top
  - TaskReopened removes from completed list
  - TaskDescriptionChanged updates completed task description
  - TaskDescriptionChanged for non-completed task (no effect)
  - TaskDeleted removes completed task
  - TaskDeleted for active task (no effect)

## 5. Slice: ReopenTask
- **Pattern:** Command (State Change)
- **Command:** ReopenTask with field `task_id`
- **Event:** TaskReopened with field `task_id`
- **Business Rule:** Reopened task placed at top (highest priority) - rationale: likely urgent if user reopens it
- **Rejection:** Task is not completed (already active)
- **GWT Scenarios (2):**
  - Reopen a completed task (goes to top)
  - Reject reopening already-active task

## 6. Slice: ChangeTaskDescription
- **Pattern:** Command (State Change)
- **Command:** ChangeTaskDescription with fields `task_id`, `new_description`
- **Event:** TaskDescriptionChanged with fields `task_id`, `description`
- **Rejection:** New description empty/blank OR identical to current (no change)
- **GWT Scenarios (4):**
  - Change a task's description
  - Reject changing to same value
  - Change description after it was previously changed
  - Reject changing back to previously changed value matching current

## 7. Slice: ReprioritizeTask
- **Pattern:** Command (State Change)
- **Command:** ReprioritizeTask with fields `task_id`, `new_position`
- **Event:** TaskReprioritized with fields `task_id`, `position`
- **Rejection:** Task dropped in same position (no change)
- **GWT Scenarios (3):**
  - Move task to higher priority (lower position number)
  - Move task to lower priority (higher position number)
  - Reject reprioritizing to same position

## 8. Slice: DeleteTask
- **Pattern:** Command (State Change)
- **Command:** DeleteTask with field `task_id`
- **Event:** TaskDeleted with field `task_id`
- **Business Rule:** Deletion permanent from UI, event stream retains full history
- **GWT Scenarios (2):**
  - Delete an active task
  - Delete a completed task

All files have been read successfully and contain the complete event model specifications for the Task Management workflow.

content.content:
7-
8-### Event Sourcing Framework: Commanded
9-
10:The application uses [Commanded](https://github.com/commanded/commanded) as its
11-CQRS/ES framework. Commanded provides the full command dispatch pipeline,
12:aggregate lifecycle management, event persistence, and projection infrastructure.
13-
14-Key components from the Commanded ecosystem:
15-
16:- **commanded** -- Core framework (command router, aggregate callbacks, middleware)
17-- **commanded_eventstore_adapter** -- Bridges Commanded to the EventStore backend
18-- **commanded_ecto_projections** -- Ecto-based read model projections
19:- **commanded_boilerplate** -- Ergonomic macros for aggregate-per-command pattern
20-  (GitHub: `jwilger/commanded_boilerplate`)
21-
22:See "Domain Architecture" below for how commands and aggregates are structured.
23-
24-### Event Store Backend: PostgreSQL (EventStore) + In-Memory for Tests
25-
26-Events are durably stored in PostgreSQL using the
27:[eventstore](https://github.com/commanded/eventstore) library with the
28-`commanded_eventstore_adapter`. This requires a dedicated PostgreSQL database
29-separate from the main Ecto application database.
30-
--
40-### Projection Mechanism: commanded_ecto_projections
41-
42-Read models are built using
43:[commanded_ecto_projections](https://github.com/commanded/commanded-ecto-projections).
44-Each read model has a corresponding Projector module that uses
45-`Commanded.Projections.Ecto` and implements `project/2` callbacks for each event
46-type it handles.
--
67-The package includes an `UUIDv7` Ecto type for seamless schema integration.
68-
69-**Generation strategy:** Task IDs are generated by the context module
70:(`TodoList.Tasks`) and passed on the command struct. The aggregate does not
71-generate IDs internally. This design enables tests to supply deterministic IDs
72-without mocking.
73-
74-## Domain Architecture
75-
76:### Aggregate Design: Aggregate-per-Command with commanded_boilerplate
77-
78:The application uses the **aggregate-per-command** pattern via the
79:[commanded_boilerplate](https://github.com/jwilger/commanded_boilerplate) library.
80:Each command module defines its own inline aggregate containing **only** the state
81-fields needed for that specific command's decision-making. There is no shared
82:"Task" aggregate module.
83-
84-**Key aspects:**
85-
86-- `use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command, identifier: :task_id` -- Sets up the command
87-  module with routing, validation middleware, and authorization middleware
88-- `inputs do ... end` -- Defines the command's input fields as a typed struct
89:- `aggregate do ... end` -- Defines the command's inline aggregate with only
90-  relevant fields and `apply/2` callbacks for event application
91:- `handle/2` -- The command handler callback that receives aggregate state and
92-  command, returns events or errors
93-- `register_command/2` -- Auto-registers the command with Commanded's router
94-
95-**Domain logic** lives directly in the command module's `handle/2` callback.
96-There is no separate domain layer and no `lib/todo_list/domain/` directory.
97-
98:**Aggregate lifecycle:** `DefaultLifespan` stops aggregate processes immediately
99-after command execution (reliability over performance). Each command execution
100-loads only the state it needs.
101-
--
139-**Parse at boundaries, trust internally:** Raw strings and integers enter the
140-system at command boundaries (e.g., from LiveView forms). Commands parse them
141-into domain types using `new/1`. Once parsed, domain types flow through events,
142:aggregates, and projections without re-validation. This ensures invalid data
143:never propagates past the boundary.
144-
145-**Ecto integration:** Each newt type auto-generates an `Ectotype` module that
146-implements `Ecto.Type`, so projection schemas use field types like
--
152-### Context Module API: TodoList.Tasks
153-
154-The context module `TodoList.Tasks` is the **single API boundary** between the
155:web layer (LiveView) and the domain layer (commands, aggregates, projections).
156-LiveView ONLY talks to the context module -- never to Commanded directly.
157-
158-**Command functions** wrap command dispatch. Each function constructs the command
--
215-1. **Mount:** Subscribe to projection update topics via Phoenix PubSub; stream
216-   initial data from `TodoList.Tasks.active_tasks()` or
217-   `TodoList.Tasks.completed_tasks()`
218:2. **User action:** Event handler calls a context command function (e.g.,
219-   `TodoList.Tasks.complete_task(task_id)`)
220-3. **Projection update:** The projector processes the resulting event, updates
221-   the database, and broadcasts via PubSub
--
227-- Commands dispatch through the context module (never directly from LiveView)
228-- The LiveView does **not** re-fetch the full list after each command; the
229-  PubSub subscription delivers granular updates
230:- For empty states and aggregate counts (e.g., `completed_count`), use separate
231-  assigns updated alongside stream operations
232-- This is a single-user app, but the subscription pattern keeps the architecture
233-  clean, testable, and consistent with how a multi-user app would work
--
240-slowest/most-integrated. GWT (Given-When-Then) scenarios from the event model
241-translate directly into test cases.
242-
243:**Layer 1 -- Aggregate Unit Tests:**
244-
245-Test command handler `handle/2` callbacks in isolation. These verify that valid
246-inputs produce correct events and that invalid inputs return appropriate errors.
--
248-
249-- Uses Commanded's **in-memory adapter** (no PostgreSQL event store)
250-- Maps directly to **Command slice** GWT scenarios:
251:  Given = prior events applied to aggregate, When = command dispatched,
252-  Then = new events emitted (or error returned)
253:- Files: `test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs`
254-
255-**Layer 2 -- Projector / Context Integration Tests:**
256-
--
268-
269-**Layer 3 -- LiveView E2E Tests:**
270-
271:Test full LiveView flows: mount the page, simulate user interactions (clicks,
272-form submissions, drag-and-drop), verify the DOM updates via stream changes.
273-
274-- Uses Commanded's **in-memory adapter** for the event store
--
334-### Warnings as Errors
335-
336-Compiler warnings are treated as errors in **all environments** -- dev, test,
337:and CI. There are no exceptions.
338-
339-- `mix compile --warnings-as-errors` must pass at all times
340:- Enforced in the `mix precommit` alias and in CI pipelines
341-- Applies to both production code and test code
342-
343-**Rationale:** Warnings indicate potential bugs, unclear code, or unused
--
360-**Rationale:** Specs serve as machine-checkable documentation, enable Dialyzer
361-to catch type errors at compile time, and make the codebase self-documenting.
362-When combined with `newt` domain types (which have specs built in), Dialyzer
363:can trace type correctness from command boundaries through aggregates to
364-projections.
365-
366:**Verification:** Dialyzer is used to verify spec correctness. See the quality
367:gates decision below for how this is integrated into the build process.
368-
369:### Pre-commit and CI Quality Gates
370-
371:Four mandatory quality gates must pass in both pre-commit hooks and the CI
372:pipeline. No PR can be merged if any gate fails.
373-
374:**Quality gates (in order):**
375-
376-1. **Compile** -- `mix compile --warnings-as-errors`
377-2. **Format** -- `mix format --check-formatted`
378:3. **Dialyzer** -- `mix dialyzer` (static type analysis, no warnings)
379-4. **Tests** -- `mix test`
380-
381:**Local enforcement:** The existing `mix precommit` alias runs all four checks
382-in sequence. Developers run this before committing or pushing.
383-
384:**CI enforcement:** GitHub Actions runs the same four checks on every PR.
385:Identical checks locally and in CI ensure no surprises.
386-
387-**Dependencies:**
388-
389-- `dialyxir` -- Added as a dev/test dependency for Dialyzer integration via
390:  Mix. Provides `mix dialyzer` task and manages PLT (Persistent Lookup Table)
391-  caching.
392-
393:**Rationale:** Automated quality gates prevent regressions, enforce consistency,
394-and catch issues before they reach main. Running the same checks in both
395:pre-commit and CI eliminates "works on my machine" failures.
396-
397-### Railway-Oriented Programming with Idiomatic `with`
398-

content.content:
# Architecture

> This is the authoritative source for the current architecture of the Todo List application.
> For the rationale behind each decision, see the ADR PRs labeled `adr` on GitHub.

## Technology Stack

### Event Sourcing Framework: Commanded

The application uses [Commanded](https://github.com/commanded/commanded) as its
CQRS/ES framework. Commanded provides the full command dispatch pipeline,
aggregate lifecycle management, event persistence, and projection infrastructure.

Key components from the Commanded ecosystem:

- **commanded** -- Core framework (command router, aggregate callbacks, middleware)
- **commanded_eventstore_adapter** -- Bridges Commanded to the EventStore backend
- **commanded_ecto_projections** -- Ecto-based read model projections
- **commanded_boilerplate** -- Ergonomic macros for aggregate-per-command pattern
  (GitHub: `jwilger/commanded_boilerplate`)

See "Domain Architecture" below for how commands and aggregates are structured.

### Event Store Backend: PostgreSQL (EventStore) + In-Memory for Tests

Events are durably stored in PostgreSQL using the
[eventstore](https://github.com/commanded/eventstore) library with the
`commanded_eventstore_adapter`. This requires a dedicated PostgreSQL database
separate from the main Ecto application database.

**Two PostgreSQL databases** (same docker-compose instance):

- **Application database** (Ecto) -- Projections / read models, standard Phoenix data
- **Event store database** (EventStore) -- Append-only event streams

**Test environment:** Tests use Commanded's built-in in-memory event store adapter.
This keeps tests fast (no database I/O), isolated (no shared state between tests),
and free of infrastructure dependencies.

### Projection Mechanism: commanded_ecto_projections

Read models are built using
[commanded_ecto_projections](https://github.com/commanded/commanded-ecto-projections).
Each read model has a corresponding Projector module that uses
`Commanded.Projections.Ecto` and implements `project/2` callbacks for each event
type it handles.

**Two projectors:**

- **TaskProjector** -- Projects events into an Ecto schema used by both the

- `.git/hooks/` contains only default Git sample hooks (pre-commit.sample, pre-push.sample, etc.) - these are not active

### 3. Dependencies and Quality Tools Status

**Currently Installed (from mix.exs):**
- `:phoenix` - Web framework
- `:phoenix_live_view` - Real-time UI
- `:ecto_sql` / `:postgrex` - Database
- `:phoenix_storybook` - Component documentation
- Standard Phoenix tooling: esbuild, tailwind, swoosh, etc.

**NOT YET INSTALLED (but documented as required):**
- `:dialyxir` - Static type checking (Dialyzer integration) - **documented as needed but not in deps**
- `:credo` - Code style/quality linting - **not referenced in project**

### 4. Mix Aliases
**Current State:** Partial implementation

File: `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list/mix.exs` (lines 79-100)

```elixir
defp aliases do

    setup: ["deps.get", "ecto.setup", "assets.setup", "assets.build"],
    "ecto.setup": ["ecto.create", "ecto.migrate", "run priv/repo/seeds.exs"],
    "ecto.reset": ["ecto.drop", "ecto.setup"],
    test: ["ecto.create --quiet", "ecto.migrate --quiet", "test"],
    "assets.setup": ["tailwind.install --if-missing", "esbuild.install --if-missing"],
    "assets.build": [
      "compile",
      "tailwind todo_list",
      "esbuild todo_list",
      "tailwind storybook",
      "esbuild storybook"
    ],
    "assets.deploy": [
      "tailwind todo_list --minify",
      "esbuild todo_list --minify",
      "phx.digest"
    ],
    precommit: ["compile --warnings-as-errors", "deps.unlock --unused", "format", "test"]
  ]
end
```

**Current `precommit` alias:**
- ✓ `compile --warnings-as-errors` - Compile with warnings as errors
- ✓ `deps.unlock --unused` - Check for unused dependencies  
- ✓ `format` - Code formatting (via `.formatter.exs`)
- ✓ `test` - Run test suite

**Missing from precommit alias (per architecture decision):**

### 6. CLI Configuration

File: `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list/mix.exs` (lines 28-32)

```elixir

    preferred_envs: [precommit: :test]
  ]
end
```

Status: ✓ **Configured** - `precommit` alias runs in test environment

### 7. Architecture Decision Documentation

File: `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (lines 369-395)

This section explicitly documents the **Pre-commit and CI Quality Gates** requirement:

**Documented Quality Gates (in order):**
1. **Compile** - `mix compile --warnings-as-errors`
2. **Format** - `mix format --check-formatted`
3. **Dialyzer** - `mix dialyzer` (static type analysis, no warnings)
4. **Tests** - `mix test`

**Notes from documentation:**
- Local enforcement via `mix precommit` alias
- CI enforcement via GitHub Actions (not yet created)
- Identical checks locally and CI to prevent "works on my machine" failures
- `dialyxir` dependency required for Dialyzer integration

Relevant ADR commits (in reverse chronological order):
- `4dfdc15` - Add design system, Catppuccin theme, and Phoenix Storybook (#40)
- `ec77b69` - ADR: Railway-Oriented Programming with Idiomatic `with` (#39)
- **`2db78e5`** - **ADR: Pre-commit and CI Quality Gates (#38)** ← RELEVANT

- `228b5cf` - ADR: Error Handling -- Structured Domain-Typed Errors via newt (#36)

File: `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list/CLAUDE.md`

References: "See AGENTS.md for all project guidelines, development environment setup, database configuration, and troubleshooting."

| `.pre-commit-config.yaml` | ❌ NOT CREATED | Pre-commit hooks not configured |

| Architecture docs | ✅ COMPLETE | docs/ARCHITECTURE.md authoritative |

### Next Steps (Based on ADR Requirements)

To complete the CI/quality gate infrastructure, you would need to:

1. **Add dialyxir dependency** to mix.exs (dev/test only)
2. **Update precommit alias** to include `dialyzer` task
3. **Create GitHub Actions workflow** (.github/workflows/ci.yml) with:

4. **Create .pre-commit-config.yaml** for local Git hooks (optional but recommended)
5. **Consider adding credo** for additional code quality checks (if desired)

All of this work is clearly documented as an approved architecture decision in the codebase.

# Slice: AddTask ## Pattern **Command** (State Change) ## Summary The user types a task description and submits it. A new task is created at the bottom of the active list with the given description and a default position. ## Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR UI["Active Task List
input field + Add button"] CMD["AddTask"]:::command EVT["TaskAdded"]:::event VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view UI -->|"description"| CMD CMD --> EVT EVT --> VIEW classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff ``` ## Command: AddTask | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | description | text | What needs to be done (non-empty) | **Triggered by:** User types in the input field and clicks [+ Add] or presses Enter. **Rejection conditions:** - Description is empty or blank. ## Event: TaskAdded | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | Unique identifier assigned to the new task | | description | text | The task description provided by the user | | position | number | Position in the list (bottom / lowest priority) | ## Wireframe ``` +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (0)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | [ Buy groceries_________________] [+ Add] | | ^--- user types here | | | | No tasks yet. Add one above! | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ After submitting: +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (0)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | | | | :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ ``` ## Read Model Impact **ActiveTaskList:** New task appears at the bottom of the list. ## GWT Scenarios ### Scenario: Add task to an empty list **Given** (prior events): - (none -- no tasks exist yet) **When** (command): - AddTask { description: "Buy groceries" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } --- ### Scenario: Add task to a list with existing tasks (new task goes to bottom) **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } **When** (command): - AddTask { description: "Fix the leaky faucet" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } --- ### Scenario: Add task when some tasks have been completed (position based on active + completed count) **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } - TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" } **When** (command): - AddTask { description: "Write thank-you notes" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-004", description: "Write thank-you notes", position: 3 } **Note:** Empty/blank description is prevented by the type system (NonEmptyString domain type), not by command rejection. No GWT scenario is needed for that case.

    45→| completed_count | number | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1), TaskDeleted (if completed, -1) | Count of completed tasks for badge |

    62→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task. |

file.content:
# Slice: ActiveTaskList

## Pattern

**View** (State View / Read Model)

## Summary

A read model that projects all events into an ordered list of active
(incomplete, non-deleted) tasks. This powers the main view of the application.

## Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    E5["TaskReprioritized"]:::event
    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view

    E1 --> VIEW
    E2 --> VIEW
    E3 --> VIEW
    E4 --> VIEW
    E5 --> VIEW
    E6 --> VIEW

    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
```

## Read Model: ActiveTaskList

### Fields

| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
| tasks | list | (all events) | Ordered list of active tasks |
| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
| tasks[].position | number | TaskAdded.position, TaskReprioritized.position, TaskReopened (top) | Current priority position |
| completed_count | number | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1), TaskDeleted (if completed, -1) | Count of completed tasks for badge |

### Filters

- Only tasks where **completed = false** AND **deleted = false**.

### Ordering

- By position ascending. Position 1 = highest priority (top of list).

### Event Processing Rules

| Event | Effect on Read Model |
|-------|---------------------|
| TaskAdded | Add new task at the bottom of the list (highest position number) |
| TaskCompleted | Remove task from active list. Increment completed_count. |
| TaskReopened | Add task back to active list at position 1 (top). Shift existing tasks down. Decrement completed_count. |
| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task. |
| TaskReprioritized | Move task to new position. Shift other tasks to accommodate. |
| TaskDeleted | Remove task from active list. If task was completed, decrement completed_count. |

### Information Completeness Check

| Display Element | Source |
|----------------|--------|
| Task description text | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
| Task order | TaskAdded.position / TaskReprioritized.position / TaskReopened (top) |
| Completed count badge | TaskCompleted / TaskReopened / TaskDeleted |
| Empty state message | Derived: tasks list is empty |

All fields trace to events. No missing data.

## Wireframe

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  My Tasks                        [Completed (3)]  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                                                    |
|  [ What needs to be done?            ] [+ Add]    |
|                                                    |
|  ::  Buy groceries                    [v] [ x ]   |
|  ::  Call the dentist                 [v] [ x ]   |
|  ::  Fix the leaky faucet            [v] [ x ]   |
|  ::  Write thank-you notes           [v] [ x ]   |
|                                                    |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

## GWT Scenarios

### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to the bottom of an empty list

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [], completed_count: 0 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }

---

### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to the bottom of a populated list

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 0 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }

---

### Scenario: TaskCompleted removes task from active list and increments completed count

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 1 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 2 }

---

### Scenario: TaskReopened adds task to top of list and decrements completed count

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 2 }], completed_count: 2 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 1 }

**Note:** The reopened task's description ("Call the dentist") must be resolved from prior events. Existing tasks shift down (positions increment by 1).

---

### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates description of an active task

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }

---

### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for a completed task does not affect active list

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist office" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }

---

### Scenario: TaskReprioritized moves task to a new position (higher priority)

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }

---

### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes an active task from the list

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 1 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }

---

### Scenario: TaskDeleted for a completed task decrements completed count but does not change active list

**Given** (current projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 2 }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-005" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }

**Note:** The projection must track whether a deleted task was completed in order to correctly adjust the completed_count.

content.content:

The task 10→disappears from the active list and appears in the completed tasks view. 11→ 12→## Diagram 13→ 14→```mermaid 15→flowchart LR 16→ UI["Active Task List

# Slice: CompleteTask ## Pattern **Command** (State Change) ## Summary The user clicks the checkbox on an active task to mark it as done. The task disappears from the active list and appears in the completed tasks view. ## Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR UI["Active Task List
checkbox on task"] CMD["CompleteTask"]:::command EVT["TaskCompleted"]:::event VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view UI -->|"task_id"| CMD CMD --> EVT EVT --> VIEW1 EVT --> VIEW2 classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff ``` ## Command: CompleteTask | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task to mark as complete | **Triggered by:** User clicks the [v] checkbox on an active task. **Rejection conditions:** - Task is already completed. ## Event: TaskCompleted | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task that was completed | ## Wireframe ``` Before: +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (2)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | | :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] | | :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ User clicks [v] on "Call the dentist": +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (3)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | | :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ Task disappears from active list, completed count increments. ``` ## Read Model Impact **ActiveTaskList:** Task removed from active list. Completed count incremented. **CompletedTaskList:** Task appears in completed list (most recent first). ## GWT Scenarios ### Scenario: Complete an active task **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } **When** (command): - CompleteTask { task_id: "task-002" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" } --- ### Scenario: Reject completing an already-completed task **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" } **When** (command): - CompleteTask { task_id: "task-001" } **Then** (error -- no events): - Error: "Task is already completed"

# Slice: CompletedTaskList

## Pattern

**View** (State View / Read Model)

## Summary

A read model that projects events into a list of completed (done, non-deleted)
tasks. This powers the completed tasks view where users can review finished work
and reopen or delete tasks.

## Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    VIEW["CompletedTaskList"]:::view

    E1 --> VIEW
    E2 --> VIEW
    E3 --> VIEW
    E4 --> VIEW
    E6 --> VIEW

    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
```

## Read Model: CompletedTaskList

### Fields

| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
| tasks | list | (filtered events) | List of completed tasks |
| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |

### Filters

- Only tasks where **completed = true** AND **deleted = false**.

### Ordering

- By completion time descending (most recently completed first). Completion
  time is derived from the TaskCompleted event timestamp.

### Event Processing Rules

| Event | Effect on Read Model |
|-------|---------------------|
| TaskAdded | No effect (new tasks are not completed). |
| TaskCompleted | Add task to completed list. |
| TaskReopened | Remove task from completed list. |
| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task (if present in list). |
| TaskDeleted | Remove task from completed list (if present). |

### Information Completeness Check

| Display Element | Source |
|----------------|--------|
| Task description text (with strikethrough) | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
| Task ordering (most recent first) | TaskCompleted event timestamp |
| Empty state | Derived: tasks list is empty |

All fields trace to events. No missing data.

## Wireframe

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  [< Back to Tasks]              Completed Tasks   |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                                                    |
|  ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~       [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
|  ~~Send the invoice~~           [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
|  ~~Order new lightbulbs~~       [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
|                                                    |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

## GWT Scenarios

### Scenario: TaskAdded has no effect on completed list

**Given** (current projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }

---

### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds task to the completed list

**Given** (current projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

**Note:** The task's description must be resolved from prior events (TaskAdded or TaskDescriptionChanged).

---

### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds most recent completion to the top

**Given** (current projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

**Note:** Most recently completed task appears first (descending completion time).

---

### Scenario: TaskReopened removes task from completed list

**Given** (current projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

---

### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates description of a completed task

**Given** (current projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries and snacks" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries and snacks" }] }

---

### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for a non-completed task does not affect completed list

**Given** (current projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet soon" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

---

### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes task from completed list

**Given** (current projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-001" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }] }

---

### Scenario: TaskDeleted for an active (non-completed) task does not affect completed list

**Given** (current projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

**When** (event to process):
- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-003" }

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }

# Slice: ReopenTask ## Pattern **Command** (State Change) ## Summary The user clicks [Reopen] on a completed task. The task is set back to incomplete and placed at the top of the active list (highest priority position). ## Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR UI["Completed Tasks View
Reopen button"] CMD["ReopenTask"]:::command EVT["TaskReopened"]:::event VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view UI -->|"task_id"| CMD CMD --> EVT EVT --> VIEW1 EVT --> VIEW2 classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff ``` ## Command: ReopenTask | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The completed task to reopen | **Triggered by:** User clicks the [Reopen] button on a task in the completed tasks view. **Rejection conditions:** - Task is not completed (already active). ## Event: TaskReopened | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task that was reopened | **Business rule:** The reopened task is placed at the top of the active list (highest priority position). This differs from newly added tasks which go to the bottom. Rationale: if a user reopens a task, it is likely urgent. ## Wireframe ``` Before (Completed Tasks View): +--------------------------------------------------+ | [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | | ~~Send the invoice~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | | ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ User clicks [Reopen] on "Send the invoice": +--------------------------------------------------+ | [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | | ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ Active Task List now shows (navigating back): +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (2)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | :: Send the invoice [v] [ x ] | | ^--- reopened task at TOP | | :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | | :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ ``` ## Read Model Impact **ActiveTaskList:** Task appears at the top (highest priority). Completed count decremented. **CompletedTaskList:** Task removed from completed list. ## GWT Scenarios ### Scenario: Reopen a completed task (goes to top of active list) **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } - TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" } **When** (command): - ReopenTask { task_id: "task-002" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" } --- ### Scenario: Reject reopening a task that is already active **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } **When** (command): - ReopenTask { task_id: "task-001" } **Then** (error -- no events): - Error: "Task is not completed"

The task's 11→description is updated. 12→ 13→## Diagram 14→ 15→```mermaid 16→flowchart LR 17→ UI["Active Task List

# Slice: ChangeTaskDescription ## Pattern **Command** (State Change) ## Summary The user clicks on a task's description text to activate inline editing, changes the text, and confirms the edit (by pressing Enter or clicking away). The task's description is updated. ## Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR UI["Active Task List
inline edit field"] CMD["ChangeTaskDescription"]:::command EVT["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view UI -->|"task_id, new_description"| CMD CMD --> EVT EVT --> VIEW1 EVT --> VIEW2 classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff ``` ## Command: ChangeTaskDescription | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task to edit | | new_description | text | The updated description (non-empty) | **Triggered by:** User clicks on a task description in the active list, edits the text inline, and confirms by pressing Enter or blurring the field. **Rejection conditions:** - New description is empty or blank. - New description is identical to the current description (no change). ## Event: TaskDescriptionChanged | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task whose description changed | | description | text | The new description | ## Wireframe ``` Before (normal display): :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] User clicks on the description text: :: [Call the dentist office____] [v] [ x ] ^--- inline editing active, cursor in text field User presses Enter or clicks away: :: Call the dentist office [v] [ x ] ^--- updated description displayed ``` ## Read Model Impact **ActiveTaskList:** Task description updated (if task is active). **CompletedTaskList:** Task description updated (if task is completed). ## GWT Scenarios ### Scenario: Change a task's description **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 } **When** (command): - ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } --- ### Scenario: Reject changing description to the same value (no change) **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 } **When** (command): - ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist" } **Then** (error -- no events): - Error: "Description is unchanged" --- ### Scenario: Change description after it was previously changed **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 } - TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } **When** (command): - ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm" } --- ### Scenario: Reject changing description back to a previously changed value that matches current **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 } - TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } **When** (command): - ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office" } **Then** (error -- no events): - Error: "Description is unchanged" **Note:** Empty/blank description is prevented by the type system (NonEmptyString domain type), not by command rejection. No GWT scenario is needed for that case.

The moved task appears at its new 71→position; other tasks shift to accommodate. 72→ 73→## GWT Scenarios 74→ 75→### Scenario: Move a task to a higher priority (lower position number) 76→ 77→**Given** (prior events): 78→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 79→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 80→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } 81→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-004", description: "Write thank-you notes", position: 4 } 82→ 83→**When** (command): 84→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-003", new_position: 1 } 85→ 86→**Then** (events produced): 87→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 } 88→ 89→--- 90→ 91→### Scenario: Move a task to a lower priority (higher position number) 92→ 93→**Given** (prior events): 94→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 95→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 96→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } 97→ 98→**When** (command): 99→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-001", new_position: 3 } 100→ 101→**Then** (events produced): 102→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-001", position: 3 } 103→ 104→--- 105→ 106→### Scenario: Reject reprioritizing to the same position (no change) 107→ 108→**Given** (prior events): 109→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 110→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 111→ 112→**When** (command): 113→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-002", new_position: 2 } 114→ 115→**Then** (error -- no events): 116→- Error: "Task is already at this position" 117→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. # Slice: ReprioritizeTask ## Pattern **Command** (State Change) ## Summary The user drags a task from one position in the active list and drops it at a new position. The task's priority changes relative to the other tasks. ## Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR UI["Active Task List
drag handle"] CMD["ReprioritizeTask"]:::command EVT["TaskReprioritized"]:::event VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view UI -->|"task_id, new_position"| CMD CMD --> EVT EVT --> VIEW classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff ``` ## Command: ReprioritizeTask | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task being moved | | new_position | number | The target position in the list | **Triggered by:** User grabs a drag handle (::) and drops the task at a new position in the active list. **Rejection conditions:** - Task dropped in the same position (no change). ## Event: TaskReprioritized | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task that was moved | | position | number | The new position in the priority-ordered list | ## Wireframe ``` Before: :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] User drags "Fix the leaky faucet" to the top: :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] <-- moved here :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] ``` ## Read Model Impact **ActiveTaskList:** Task order updated. The moved task appears at its new position; other tasks shift to accommodate. ## GWT Scenarios ### Scenario: Move a task to a higher priority (lower position number) **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-004", description: "Write thank-you notes", position: 4 } **When** (command): - ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-003", new_position: 1 } **Then** (events produced): - TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 } --- ### Scenario: Move a task to a lower priority (higher position number) **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } **When** (command): - ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-001", new_position: 3 } **Then** (events produced): - TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-001", position: 3 } --- ### Scenario: Reject reprioritizing to the same position (no change) **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } **When** (command): - ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-002", new_position: 2 } **Then** (error -- no events): - Error: "Task is already at this position"
