1→# Slice: DeleteTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks the delete button on a task (from either the active list or the 10→completed tasks view). A confirmation dialog appears. If the user confirms, the 11→task is permanently removed from all views. 12→ 13→## Diagram 14→ 15→```mermaid 16→flowchart LR 17→ UI1["Active Task List
delete button"] 18→ UI2["Completed Tasks View
delete button"] 19→ DIALOG["Confirmation Dialog"] 20→ CMD["DeleteTask"]:::command 21→ EVT["TaskDeleted"]:::event 22→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 23→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 24→ 25→ UI1 -->|"click x"| DIALOG 26→ UI2 -->|"click x"| DIALOG 27→ DIALOG -->|"confirm, task_id"| CMD 28→ CMD --> EVT 29→ EVT --> VIEW1 30→ EVT --> VIEW2 31→ 32→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 33→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 34→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 35→``` 36→ 37→## Command: DeleteTask 38→ 39→| Field | Type | Description | 40→|-------|------|-------------| 41→| task_id | identifier | The task to delete | 42→ 43→**Triggered by:** User clicks [x] on any task (active or completed), then 44→clicks [Delete] in the confirmation dialog. 45→ 46→**Rejection conditions:** 47→- User clicks [Cancel] in the confirmation dialog (command is never issued). 48→ 49→## Event: TaskDeleted 50→ 51→| Field | Type | Description | 52→|-------|------|-------------| 53→| task_id | identifier | The task that was deleted | 54→ 55→**Business rule:** Deletion is permanent from the UI perspective. The event 56→stream retains the full history, but the UI provides no way to restore a 57→deleted task. 58→ 59→## Wireframe 60→ 61→``` 62→User clicks [x] on "Fix the leaky faucet": 63→ 64→+--------------------------------------------------+ 65→| | 66→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 67→| | Delete Task? | | 68→| | | | 69→| | "Fix the leaky faucet" will be | | 70→| | permanently removed. | | 71→| | | | 72→| | [Cancel] [Delete] | | 73→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 74→| | 75→+--------------------------------------------------+ 76→ 77→If user clicks [Delete], the task disappears from the list. 78→If user clicks [Cancel], nothing changes. 79→``` 80→ 81→## Read Model Impact 82→ 83→**ActiveTaskList:** Task removed (if it was active). Remaining tasks may shift 84→positions. 85→**CompletedTaskList:** Task removed (if it was completed). 86→ 87→## GWT Scenarios 88→ 89→### Scenario: Delete an active task 90→ 91→**Given** (prior events): 92→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 93→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 94→ 95→**When** (command): 96→- DeleteTask { task_id: "task-002" } 97→ 98→**Then** (events produced): 99→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" } 100→ 101→--- 102→ 103→### Scenario: Delete a completed task 104→ 105→**Given** (prior events): 106→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 107→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" } 108→ 109→**When** (command): 110→- DeleteTask { task_id: "task-001" } 111→ 112→**Then** (events produced): 113→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-001" } 114→ 115→**Note:** The confirmation dialog is a UI concern. If the user clicks Cancel, the command is never issued, so there is no rejection scenario at the command level. Once the DeleteTask command is issued (after confirmation), it always succeeds. 116→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. # Slice: DeleteTask ## Pattern **Command** (State Change) ## Summary The user clicks the delete button on a task (from either the active list or the completed tasks view). A confirmation dialog appears. If the user confirms, the task is permanently removed from all views. ## Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR UI1["Active Task List
delete button"] UI2["Completed Tasks View
delete button"] DIALOG["Confirmation Dialog"] CMD["DeleteTask"]:::command EVT["TaskDeleted"]:::event VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view UI1 -->|"click x"| DIALOG UI2 -->|"click x"| DIALOG DIALOG -->|"confirm, task_id"| CMD CMD --> EVT EVT --> VIEW1 EVT --> VIEW2 classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff ``` ## Command: DeleteTask | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task to delete | **Triggered by:** User clicks [x] on any task (active or completed), then clicks [Delete] in the confirmation dialog. **Rejection conditions:** - User clicks [Cancel] in the confirmation dialog (command is never issued). ## Event: TaskDeleted | Field | Type | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | task_id | identifier | The task that was deleted | **Business rule:** Deletion is permanent from the UI perspective. The event stream retains the full history, but the UI provides no way to restore a deleted task. ## Wireframe ``` User clicks [x] on "Fix the leaky faucet": +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | +--------------------------------------------+ | | | Delete Task? | | | | | | | | "Fix the leaky faucet" will be | | | | permanently removed. | | | | | | | | [Cancel] [Delete] | | | +--------------------------------------------+ | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ If user clicks [Delete], the task disappears from the list. If user clicks [Cancel], nothing changes. ``` ## Read Model Impact **ActiveTaskList:** Task removed (if it was active). Remaining tasks may shift positions. **CompletedTaskList:** Task removed (if it was completed). ## GWT Scenarios ### Scenario: Delete an active task **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } **When** (command): - DeleteTask { task_id: "task-002" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" } --- ### Scenario: Delete a completed task **Given** (prior events): - TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } - TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" } **When** (command): - DeleteTask { task_id: "task-001" } **Then** (events produced): - TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-001" } **Note:** The confirmation dialog is a UI concern. If the user clicks Cancel, the command is never issued, so there is no rejection scenario at the command level. Once the DeleteTask command is issued (after confirmation), it always succeeds.

defmodule TodoList.MixProject do
  use Mix.Project

  def project do
    [
      app: :todo_list,
      version: "0.1.0",
      elixir: "~> 1.15",
      elixirc_paths: elixirc_paths(Mix.env()),
      start_permanent: Mix.env() == :prod,
      aliases: aliases(),
      deps: deps(),
      compilers: [:phoenix_live_view] ++ Mix.compilers(),
      listeners: [Phoenix.CodeReloader]
    ]
  end

  # Configuration for the OTP application.
  #
  # Type `mix help compile.app` for more information.
  def application do
    [
      mod: {TodoList.Application, []},
      extra_applications: [:logger, :runtime_tools]
    ]
  end

  def cli do
    [
      preferred_envs: [precommit: :test]
    ]
  end

  # Specifies which paths to compile per environment.
  defp elixirc_paths(:test), do: ["lib", "test/support"]
  defp elixirc_paths(_), do: ["lib"]

  # Specifies your project dependencies.
  #
  # Type `mix help deps` for examples and options.
  defp deps do
    [
      {:phoenix, "~> 1.8.3"},
      {:phoenix_ecto, "~> 4.5"},
      {:ecto_sql, "~> 3.13"},
      {:postgrex, ">= 0.0.0"},
      {:phoenix_html, "~> 4.1"},
      {:phoenix_live_reload, "~> 1.2", only: :dev},
      {:phoenix_live_view, "~> 1.1.0"},
      {:lazy_html, ">= 0.1.0", only: :test},
      {:phoenix_live_dashboard, "~> 0.8.3"},
      {:esbuild, "~> 0.10", runtime: Mix.env() == :dev},
      {:tailwind, "~> 0.3", runtime: Mix.env() == :dev},
      {:heroicons,
       github: "tailwindlabs/heroicons",
       tag: "v2.2.0",
       sparse: "optimized",
       app: false,
       compile: false,
       depth: 1},
      {:swoosh, "~> 1.16"},
      {:req, "~> 0.5"},
      {:telemetry_metrics, "~> 1.0"},
      {:telemetry_poller, "~> 1.0"},
      {:gettext, "~> 1.0"},
      {:jason, "~> 1.2"},
      {:dns_cluster, "~> 0.2.0"},
      {:bandit, "~> 1.5"},
      {:phoenix_storybook, "~> 0.9.0", only: [:dev, :test]}
    ]
  end

  # Aliases are shortcuts or tasks specific to the current project.
  # For example, to install project dependencies and perform other setup tasks, run:
  #
  #     $ mix setup
  #
  # See the documentation for `Mix` for more info on aliases.
  defp aliases do
    [
      setup: ["deps.get", "ecto.setup", "assets.setup", "assets.build"],
      "ecto.setup": ["ecto.create", "ecto.migrate", "run priv/repo/seeds.exs"],
      "ecto.reset": ["ecto.drop", "ecto.setup"],
      test: ["ecto.create --quiet", "ecto.migrate --quiet", "test"],
      "assets.setup": ["tailwind.install --if-missing", "esbuild.install --if-missing"],
      "assets.build": [
        "compile",
        "tailwind todo_list",
        "esbuild todo_list",
        "tailwind storybook",
        "esbuild storybook"
      ],
      "assets.deploy": [
        "tailwind todo_list --minify",
        "esbuild todo_list --minify",
        "phx.digest"
      ],
      precommit: ["compile --warnings-as-errors", "deps.unlock --unused", "format", "test"]
    ]
  end
end

1→This is a web application written using the Phoenix web framework. 2→ 3→## Development Environment 4→ 5→This project uses a Nix-based development environment for reproducible Elixir/Phoenix development. 6→ 7→The dev shell should already be loaded via direnv. If not, run `nix develop`. 8→ 9→### Running utilities not in the dev shell 10→ 11→Use `nix shell` to temporarily run utilities: 12→```bash 13→nix shell nixpkgs# -c 14→``` 15→ 16→### Universal Tools Available 17→ 18→- `just` - Task runner (see Justfile if present) 19→- `glow` - Render markdown in terminal (`glow README.md`) 20→- `jq` - JSON processing 21→- `git-spice` - Stacked PR workflow (`gs` commands) 22→- `pre-commit` - Git hooks 23→ 24→### Language-Specific Tools 25→ 26→**Elixir/Phoenix:** 27→- `elixir` - Elixir runtime 28→- `mix` - Build tool and task runner 29→- `iex` - Interactive Elixir shell 30→- `elixir-ls` - Language server for IDE integration 31→- `psql` - PostgreSQL client (version 17) 32→ 33→**Container Tools:** 34→- `docker` - Container runtime 35→- `docker-compose` - Multi-container orchestration 36→ 37→**Note on Language Servers:** 38→Consider using [Lexical](https://github.com/lexical-lsp/lexical) instead of ElixirLS for better performance and features. Lexical can be built from source within this Nix environment. 39→ 40→## Database Setup 41→ 42→PostgreSQL 17 runs in Docker via docker-compose. 43→ 44→**Connection parameters:** 45→- **Host:** localhost 46→- **Port:** 5432 47→- **User:** postgres 48→- **Password:** postgres 49→- **Database:** todo_list_dev 50→ 51→Phoenix database URL format: 52→``` 53→DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/todo_list_dev 54→``` 55→ 56→**Docker commands:** 57→```bash 58→# Start PostgreSQL 59→docker-compose up -d 60→ 61→# Check status 62→docker-compose ps 63→ 64→# View logs 65→docker-compose logs -f postgres 66→ 67→# Stop PostgreSQL 68→docker-compose down 69→ 70→# Stop and remove volumes (DESTRUCTIVE) 71→docker-compose down -v 72→``` 73→ 74→## Common Mix Tasks 75→ 76→```bash 77→# Get dependencies 78→mix deps.get 79→ 80→# Compile project 81→mix compile 82→ 83→# Run tests 84→mix test 85→ 86→# Run tests with coverage 87→mix migrate 94→ 95→# Code quality 96→mix format # Format code 97→mix credo # Static analysis (if added) 98→mix dialyzer # Type checking (if added) 99→ 100→# Interactive shell 101→iex -S mix # Start IEx with project loaded 102→iex -S mix phx.server # Start server with IEx shell 103→``` 104→ 105→## Git Workflow 106→ 107→This project uses git-spice for stacked PRs: 108→- `gs repo sync` - Sync with remote 109→- `gs stack submit` - Submit all stacked PRs 110→- `gs branch create ` - Create new stacked branch 111→- `gs upstack onto ` - Restack commits onto branch 112→ 113→## Commit Message Guidelines 114→ 115→Use commitizen-style commit messages: 116→ 117→``` 118→(): 119→ 120→ 121→``` 122→ 123→**Types:** feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, perf, test, build, ci, chore, revert 124→ 125→**Examples:** 126→- `feat(users): add user registration endpoint` 127→- `fix(auth): resolve token expiration bug` 128→- `docs(readme): update setup instructions` 129→ 130→Commit messages should explain the **WHY**, not just what changed. 131→ 132→## Troubleshooting 133→ 134→### Dev shell not loading 135→```bash 136→# Check direnv status 137→direnv status 138→ 139→# Force reload 140→direnv allow 141→ 142→# Or manually enter shell 143→nix develop 144→``` 145→ 146→### Mix/Hex issues 147→```bash 148→# Clean Mix cache 149→rm -rf .nix-shell/mix .nix-shell/hex 150→ 151→# Reload shell (will reinstall hex/rebar3) 152→direnv reload 153→ 154→# Or manually reinstall 155→mix local.hex --force 156→mix local.rebar --force 157→``` 158→ 159→### PostgreSQL connection issues 160→```bash 161→# Check if PostgreSQL is running 162→docker-compose ps 163→ 164→# Check PostgreSQL logs 165→docker-compose logs postgres 166→ 167→# Restart PostgreSQL 168→docker-compose restart postgres 169→``` 170→ 171→### Dependency conflicts 172→```bash 173→# Clean all dependencies 174→mix deps.clean --all 175→ 176→# Get fresh dependencies 177→mix deps.get 178→ 179→# Compile everything 180→mix deps.compile 181→``` 182→ 183→## Additional Resources 184→ 185→- [Elixir Documentation](https://elixir-lang.org/docs.html) 186→- [Phoenix Framework](https://www.phoenixframework.org/) 187→- [Phoenix LiveView](https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix_live_view/) 188→- [Ecto Documentation](https://hexdocs.pm/ecto/) 189→- [Elixir Forum](https://elixirforum.com/) 190→ 191→## Project guidelines 192→ 193→- Use `mix precommit` alias when you are done with all changes and fix any pending issues 194→- Use the already included and available `:req` (`Req`) library for HTTP requests, **avoid** `:httpoison`, `:tesla`, and `:httpc`. Req is included by default and is the preferred HTTP client for Phoenix apps 195→ 196→### Phoenix v1.8 guidelines 197→ 198→- **Always** begin your LiveView templates with `` which wraps all inner content 199→- The `MyAppWeb.Layouts` module is aliased in the `my_app_web.ex` file, so you can use it without needing to alias it again 200→- Anytime you run into errors with no `current_scope` assign: 201→ - You failed to follow the Authenticated Routes guidelines, or you failed to pass `current_scope` to `` 202→ - **Always** fix the `current_scope` error by moving your routes to the proper `live_session` and ensure you pass `current_scope` as needed 203→- Phoenix v1.8 moved the `<.flash_group>` component to the `Layouts` module. You are **forbidden** from calling `<.flash_group>` outside of the `layouts.ex` module 204→- Out of the box, `core_components.ex` imports an `<.icon name="hero-x-mark" class="w-5 h-5"/>` component for for hero icons. **Always** use the `<.icon>` component for icons, **never** use `Heroicons` modules or similar 205→- **Always** use the imported `<.input>` component for form inputs from `core_components.ex` when available. `<.input>` is imported and using it will save steps and prevent errors 206→- If you override the default input classes (`<.input class="myclass px-2 py-1 rounded-lg">)`) class with your own values, no default classes are inherited, so your 207→custom classes must fully style the input 208→ 209→### JS and CSS guidelines 210→ 211→- **Use Tailwind CSS classes and custom CSS rules** to create polished, responsive, and visually stunning interfaces. 212→- Tailwindcss v4 **no longer needs a tailwind.config.js** and uses a new import syntax in `app.css`: 213→ 214→ @import "tailwindcss" source(none); 215→ @source "../css"; 216→ @source "../js"; 217→ @source "../../lib/my_app_web"; 218→ 219→- **Always use and maintain this import syntax** in the app.css file for projects generated with `phx.new` 220→- **Never** use `@apply` when writing raw css 221→- **Always** manually write your own tailwind-based components instead of using daisyUI for a unique, world-class design 222→- Out of the box **only the app.js and app.css bundles are supported** 223→ - You cannot reference an external vendor'd script `src` or link `href` in the layouts 224→ - You must import the vendor deps into app.js and app.css to use them 225→ - **Never write inline tags within templates** 226→ 227→### UI/UX & design guidelines 228→ 229→- **Produce world-class UI designs** with a focus on usability, aesthetics, and modern design principles 230→- Implement **subtle micro-interactions** (e.g., button hover effects, and smooth transitions) 231→- Ensure **clean typography, spacing, and layout balance** for a refined, premium look 232→- Focus on **delightful details** like hover effects, loading states, and smooth page transitions 233→ 234→ 235→ 236→ 237→ 238→## Elixir guidelines 239→ 240→- Elixir lists **do not support index based access via the access syntax** 241→ 242→ **Never do this (invalid)**: 243→ 244→ i = 0 245→ mylist = ["blue", "green"] 246→ mylist[i] 247→ 248→ Instead, **always** use `Enum.at`, pattern matching, or `List` for index based list access, ie: 249→ 250→ i = 0 251→ mylist = ["blue", "green"] 252→ Enum.at(mylist, i) 253→ 254→- Elixir variables are immutable, but can be rebound, so for block expressions like `if`, `case`, `cond`, etc 255→ you *must* bind the result of the expression to a variable if you want to use it and you CANNOT rebind the result inside the expression, ie: 256→ 257→ # INVALID: we are rebinding inside the `if` and the result never gets assigned 258→ if connected?(socket) do 259→ socket = assign(socket, :val, val) 260→ end 261→ 262→ # VALID: we rebind the result of the `if` to a new variable 263→ socket = 264→ if connected?(socket) do 265→ assign(socket, :val, val) 266→ end 267→ 268→- **Never** nest multiple modules in the same file as it can cause cyclic dependencies and compilation errors 269→- **Never** use map access syntax (`changeset[:field]`) on structs as they do not implement the Access behaviour by default. For regular structs, you **must** access the fields directly, such as `my_struct.field` or use higher level APIs that are available on the struct if they exist, `Ecto.Changeset.get_field/2` for changesets 270→- Elixir's standard library has everything necessary for date and time manipulation. Familiarize yourself with the common `Time`, `Date`, `DateTime`, and `Calendar` interfaces by accessing their documentation as necessary. **Never** install additional dependencies unless asked or for date/time parsing (which you can use the `date_time_parser` package) 271→- Don't use `String.to_atom/1` on user input (memory leak risk) 272→- Predicate function names should not start with `is_` and should end in a question mark. Names like `is_thing` should be reserved for guards 273→- Elixir's builtin OTP primitives like `DynamicSupervisor` and `Registry`, require names in the child spec, such as `{DynamicSupervisor, name: MyApp.MyDynamicSup}`, then you can use `DynamicSupervisor.start_child(MyApp.MyDynamicSup, child_spec)` 274→- Use `Task.async_stream(collection, callback, options)` for concurrent enumeration with back-pressure. The majority of times you will want to pass `timeout: :infinity` as option 275→ 276→## Mix guidelines 277→ 278→- Read the docs and options before using tasks (by using `mix help task_name`) 279→- To debug test failures, run tests in a specific file with `mix test test/my_test.exs` or run all previously failed tests with `mix test --failed` 280→- `mix deps.clean --all` is **almost never needed**. **Avoid** using it unless you have good reason 281→ 282→## Test guidelines 283→ 284→- **Always use `start_supervised!/1`** to start processes in tests as it guarantees cleanup between tests 285→- **Avoid** `Process.sleep/1` and `Process.alive?/1` in tests 286→ - Instead of sleeping to wait for a process to finish, **always** use `Process.monitor/1` and assert on the DOWN message: 287→ 288→ ref = Process.monitor(pid) 289→ assert_receive {:DOWN, ^ref, :process, ^pid, :normal} 290→ 291→ - Instead of sleeping to synchronize before the next call, **always** use `_ = :sys.get_state/1` to ensure the process has handled prior messages 292→ 293→ 294→ 295→## Phoenix guidelines 296→ 297→- Remember Phoenix router `scope` blocks include an optional alias which is prefixed for all routes within the scope. **Always** be mindful of this when creating routes within a scope to avoid duplicate module prefixes. 298→ 299→- You **never** need to create your own `alias` for route definitions! The `scope` provides the alias, ie: 300→ 301→ scope "/admin", AppWeb.Admin do 302→ pipe_through :browser 303→ 304→ live "/users", UserLive, :index 305→ end 306→ 307→ the UserLive route would point to the `AppWeb.Admin.UserLive` module 308→ 309→- `Phoenix.View` no longer is needed or included with Phoenix, don't use it 310→ 311→ 312→ 313→## Ecto Guidelines 314→ 315→- **Always** preload Ecto associations in queries when they'll be accessed in templates, ie a message that needs to reference the `message.user.email` 316→- Remember `import Ecto.Query` and other supporting modules when you write `seeds.exs` 317→- `Ecto.Schema` fields always use the `:string` type, even for `:text`, columns, ie: `field :name, :string` 318→- `Ecto.Changeset.validate_number/2` **DOES NOT SUPPORT the `:allow_nil` option**. By default, Ecto validations only run if a change for the given field exists and the change value is not nil, so such as option is never needed 319→- You **must** use `Ecto.Changeset.get_field(changeset, :field)` to access changeset fields 320→- Fields which are set programatically, such as `user_id`, must not be listed in `cast` calls or similar for security purposes. Instead they must be explicitly set when creating the struct 321→- **Always** invoke `mix ecto.gen.migration migration_name_using_underscores` when generating migration files, so the correct timestamp and conventions are applied 322→ 323→ 324→ 325→## Phoenix HTML guidelines 326→ 327→- Phoenix templates **always** use `~H` or .html.heex files (known as HEEx), **never** use `~E` 328→- **Always** use the imported `Phoenix.Component.form/1` and `Phoenix.Component.inputs_for/1` function to build forms. **Never** use `Phoenix.HTML.form_for` or `Phoenix.HTML.inputs_for` as they are outdated 329→- When building forms **always** use the already imported `Phoenix.Component.to_form/2` (`assign(socket, form: to_form(...))` and `<.form for={@form} id="msg-form">`), then access those forms in the template via `@form[:field]` 330→- **Always** add unique DOM IDs to key elements (like forms, buttons, etc) when writing templates, these IDs can later be used in tests (`<.form for={@form} id="product-form">`) 331→- For "app wide" template imports, you can import/alias into the `my_app_web.ex`'s `html_helpers` block, so they will be available to all LiveViews, LiveComponent's, and all modules that do `use MyAppWeb, :html` (replace "my_app" by the actual app name) 332→ 333→- Elixir supports `if/else` but **does NOT support `if/else if` or `if/elsif`**. **Never use `else if` or `elseif` in Elixir**, **always** use `cond` or `case` for multiple conditionals. 334→ 335→ **Never do this (invalid)**: 336→ 337→ <%= if condition do %> 338→ ... 339→ <% else if other_condition %> 340→ ... 341→ <% end %> 342→ 343→ Instead **always** do this: 344→ 345→ <%= cond do %> 346→ <% condition -> %> 347→ ... 348→ <% condition2 -> %> 349→ ... 350→ <% true -> %> 351→ ... 352→ <% end %> 353→ 354→- HEEx require special tag annotation if you want to insert literal curly's like `{` or `}`. If you want to show a textual code snippet on the page in a `
` or `` block you *must* annotate the parent tag with `phx-no-curly-interpolation`:
   355→
   356→      
   357→        let obj = {key: "val"}
   358→      
   359→
   360→  Within `phx-no-curly-interpolation` annotated tags, you can use `{` and `}` without escaping them, and dynamic Elixir expressions can still be used with `<%= ... %>` syntax
   361→
   362→- HEEx class attrs support lists, but you must **always** use list `[...]` syntax. You can use the class list syntax to conditionally add classes, **always do this for multiple class values**:
   363→

   370→
   371→  and **always** wrap `if`'s inside `{...}` expressions with parens, like done above (`if(@other_condition, do: "...", else: "...")`)
   372→
   373→  and **never** do this, since it's invalid (note the missing `[` and `]`):
   374→
   375→       ...
   379→      => Raises compile syntax error on invalid HEEx attr syntax
   380→
   381→- **Never** use `<% Enum.each %>` or non-for comprehensions for generating template content, instead **always** use `<%= for item <- @collection do %>`
   382→- HEEx HTML comments use `<%!-- comment --%>`. **Always** use the HEEx HTML comment syntax for template comments (`<%!-- comment --%>`)
   383→- HEEx allows interpolation via `{...}` and `<%= ... %>`, but the `<%= %>` **only** works within tag bodies. **Always** use the `{...}` syntax for interpolation within tag attributes, and for interpolation of values within tag bodies. **Always** interpolate block constructs (if, cond, case, for) within tag bodies using `<%= ... %>`.

   404→## Phoenix LiveView guidelines
   405→
   406→- **Never** use the deprecated `live_redirect` and `live_patch` functions, instead **always** use the `<.link navigate={href}>` and  `<.link patch={href}>` in templates, and `push_navigate` and `push_patch` functions LiveViews
   407→- **Avoid LiveComponent's** unless you have a strong, specific need for them
   408→- LiveViews should be named like `AppWeb.WeatherLive`, with a `Live` suffix. When you go to add LiveView routes to the router, the default `:browser` scope is **already aliased** with the `AppWeb` module, so you can just do `live "/weather", WeatherLive`
   409→
   410→### LiveView streams
   411→
   412→- **Always** use LiveView streams for collections for assigning regular lists to avoid memory ballooning and runtime termination with the following operations:
   413→  - basic append of N items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])`
   414→  - resetting stream with new items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], reset: true)` (e.g. for filtering items)
   415→  - prepend to stream - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], at: -1)`
   416→  - deleting items - `stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg)`
   417→
   418→- When using the `stream/3` interfaces in the LiveView, the LiveView template must 1) always set `phx-update="stream"` on the parent element, with a DOM id on the parent element like `id="messages"` and 2) consume the `@streams.stream_name` collection and use the id as the DOM id for each child. For a call like `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])` in the LiveView, the template would be:
   419→
   420→      

   421→        

   422→          {msg.text}
   423→        

   424→      

   425→
   426→- LiveView streams are *not* enumerable, so you cannot use `Enum.filter/2` or `Enum.reject/2` on them. Instead, if you want to filter, prune, or refresh a list of items on the UI, you **must refetch the data and re-stream the entire stream collection, passing reset: true**:
   427→
   428→      def handle_event("filter", %{"filter" => filter}, socket) do
   429→        # re-fetch the messages based on the filter
   430→        messages = list_messages(filter)
   431→
   432→        {:noreply,
   433→         socket
   434→         |> assign(:messages_empty?, messages == [])
   435→         # reset the stream with the new messages
   436→         |> stream(:messages, messages, reset: true)}
   437→      end
   438→
   439→- LiveView streams *do not support counting or empty states*. If you need to display a count, you must track it using a separate assign. For empty states, you can use Tailwind classes:
   440→
   441→      

   442→        
No tasks yet

   443→        

   444→          {task.name}
   445→        

   446→      

   447→
   448→  The above only works if the empty state is the only HTML block alongside the stream for-comprehension.
   449→
   450→- When updating an assign that should change content inside any streamed item(s), you MUST re-stream the items
   451→  along with the updated assign:
   452→
   453→      def handle_event("edit_message", %{"message_id" => message_id}, socket) do
   454→        message = Chat.get_message!(message_id)
   455→        edit_form = to_form(Chat.change_message(message, %{content: message.content}))
   456→
   457→        # re-insert message so @editing_message_id toggle logic takes effect for that stream item
   458→        {:noreply,
   459→         socket
   460→         |> stream_insert(:messages, message)
   461→         |> assign(:editing_message_id, String.to_integer(message_id))
   462→         |> assign(:edit_form, edit_form)}
   463→      end
   464→
   465→  And in the template:
   466→
   467→      

   468→        

   469→          {message.username}
   470→          <%= if @editing_message_id == message.id do %>
   471→            <%!-- Edit mode --%>
   472→            <.form for={@edit_form} id="edit-form-#{message.id}" phx-submit="save_edit">
   473→              ...
   474→            
   475→          <% end %>
   476→        

   477→      

   478→
   479→- **Never** use the deprecated `phx-update="append"` or `phx-update="prepend"` for collections
   480→
   481→### LiveView JavaScript interop
   482→
   483→- Remember anytime you use `phx-hook="MyHook"` and that JS hook manages its own DOM, you **must** also set the `phx-update="ignore"` attribute
   484→- **Always** provide an unique DOM id alongside `phx-hook` otherwise a compiler error will be raised
   485→
   486→LiveView hooks come in two flavors, 1) colocated js hooks for "inline" scripts defined inside HEEx,
   487→and 2) external `phx-hook` annotations where JavaScript object literals are defined and passed to the `LiveSocket` constructor.
   488→
   489→#### Inline colocated js hooks
   490→
   491→**Never** write raw embedded `
   508→
   509→- colocated hooks are automatically integrated into the app.js bundle
   510→- colocated hooks names **MUST ALWAYS** start with a `.` prefix, i.e. `.PhoneNumber`
   511→
   512→#### External phx-hook
   513→
   514→External JS hooks (`
`) must be placed in `assets/js/` and passed to the
   515→LiveSocket constructor:
   516→
   517→    const MyHook = {
   518→      mounted() { ... }
   519→    }
   520→    let liveSocket = new LiveSocket("/live", Socket, {
   521→      hooks: { MyHook }
   522→    });
   523→
   524→#### Pushing events between client and server
   525→
   526→Use LiveView's `push_event/3` when you need to push events/data to the client for a phx-hook to handle.
   527→**Always** return or rebind the socket on `push_event/3` when pushing events:
   528→
   529→    # re-bind socket so we maintain event state to be pushed
   530→    socket = push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})
   531→
   532→    # or return the modified socket directly:
   533→    def handle_event("some_event", _, socket) do
   534→      {:noreply, push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})}
   535→    end
   536→
   537→Pushed events can then be picked up in a JS hook with `this.handleEvent`:
   538→
   539→    mounted() {
   540→      this.handleEvent("my_event", data => console.log("from server:", data));
   541→    }
   542→
   543→Clients can also push an event to the server and receive a reply with `this.pushEvent`:
   544→
   545→    mounted() {
   546→      this.el.addEventListener("click", e => {
   547→        this.pushEvent("my_event", { one: 1 }, reply => console.log("got reply from server:", reply));
   548→      })
   549→    }
   550→
   551→Where the server handled it via:
   552→
   553→    def handle_event("my_event", %{"one" => 1}, socket) do
   554→      {:reply, %{two: 2}, socket}
   555→    end
   556→
   557→### LiveView tests
   558→
   559→- `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` module and `LazyHTML` (included) for making your assertions
   560→- Form tests are driven by `Phoenix.LiveViewTest`'s `render_submit/2` and `render_change/2` functions
   561→- Come up with a step-by-step test plan that splits major test cases into small, isolated files. You may start with simpler tests that verify content exists, gradually add interaction tests
   562→- **Always reference the key element IDs you added in the LiveView templates in your tests** for `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` functions like `element/2`, `has_element/2`, selectors, etc
   563→- **Never** tests again raw HTML, **always** use `element/2`, `has_element/2`, and similar: `assert has_element?(view, "#my-form")`
   564→- Instead of relying on testing text content, which can change, favor testing for the presence of key elements
   565→- Focus on testing outcomes rather than implementation details
   566→- Be aware that `Phoenix.Component` functions like `<.form>` might produce different HTML than expected. Test against the output HTML structure, not your mental model of what you expect it to be
   567→- When facing test failures with element selectors, add debug statements to print the actual HTML, but use `LazyHTML` selectors to limit the output, ie:
   568→
   569→      html = render(view)
   570→      document = LazyHTML.from_fragment(html)
   571→      matches = LazyHTML.filter(document, "your-complex-selector")
   572→      IO.inspect(matches, label: "Matches")
   573→
   574→### Form handling
   575→
   576→#### Creating a form from params
   577→
   578→If you want to create a form based on `handle_event` params:
   579→
   580→    def handle_event("submitted", params, socket) do
   581→      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(params))}
   582→    end
   583→
   584→When you pass a map to `to_form/1`, it assumes said map contains the form params, which are expected to have string keys.
   585→
   586→You can also specify a name to nest the params:
   587→
   588→    def handle_event("submitted", %{"user" => user_params}, socket) do
   589→      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(user_params, as: :user))}
   590→    end
   591→
   592→#### Creating a form from changesets
   593→
   594→When using changesets, the underlying data, form params, and errors are retrieved from it. The `:as` option is automatically computed too. E.g. if you have a user schema:
   595→
   596→    defmodule MyApp.Users.User do
   597→      use Ecto.Schema
   598→      ...
   599→    end
   600→
   601→And then you create a changeset that you pass to `to_form`:
   602→
   603→    %MyApp.Users.User{}
   604→    |> Ecto.Changeset.change()
   605→    |> to_form()
   606→
   607→Once the form is submitted, the params will be available under `%{"user" => user_params}`.
   608→
   609→In the template, the form form assign can be passed to the `<.form>` function component:
   610→
   611→    <.form for={@form} id="todo-form" phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save">
   612→      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
   613→    
   614→
   615→Always give the form an explicit, unique DOM ID, like `id="todo-form"`.
   616→
   617→#### Avoiding form errors
   618→
   619→**Always** use a form assigned via `to_form/2` in the LiveView, and the `<.input>` component in the template. In the template **always access forms this**:
   620→
   621→    <%!-- ALWAYS do this (valid) --%>
   622→    <.form for={@form} id="my-form">
   623→      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
   624→    
   625→
   626→And **never** do this:
   627→
   628→    <%!-- NEVER do this (invalid) --%>
   629→    <.form for={@changeset} id="my-form">
   630→      <.input field={@changeset[:field]} type="text" />
   631→    
   632→
   633→- You are FORBIDDEN from accessing the changeset in the template as it will cause errors
   634→- **Never** use `<.form let={f} ...>` in the template, instead **always use `<.form for={@form} ...>`**, then drive all form references from the form assign as in `@form[:field]`. The UI should **always** be driven by a `to_form/2` assigned in the LiveView module that is derived from a changeset
   635→
   636→
   637→

This is a web application written using the Phoenix web framework. ## Development Environment This project uses a Nix-based development environment for reproducible Elixir/Phoenix development. The dev shell should already be loaded via direnv. If not, run `nix develop`. ### Running utilities not in the dev shell Use `nix shell` to temporarily run utilities: ```bash nix shell nixpkgs# -c ``` ### Universal Tools Available - `just` - Task runner (see Justfile if present) - `glow` - Render markdown in terminal (`glow README.md`) - `jq` - JSON processing - `git-spice` - Stacked PR workflow (`gs` commands) - `pre-commit` - Git hooks ### Language-Specific Tools **Elixir/Phoenix:** - `elixir` - Elixir runtime - `mix` - Build tool and task runner - `iex` - Interactive Elixir shell - `elixir-ls` - Language server for IDE integration - `psql` - PostgreSQL client (version 17) **Container Tools:** - `docker` - Container runtime - `docker-compose` - Multi-container orchestration **Note on Language Servers:** Consider using [Lexical](https://github.com/lexical-lsp/lexical) instead of ElixirLS for better performance and features. Lexical can be built from source within this Nix environment. ## Database Setup PostgreSQL 17 runs in Docker via docker-compose. **Connection parameters:** - **Host:** localhost - **Port:** 5432 - **User:** postgres - **Password:** postgres - **Database:** todo_list_dev Phoenix database URL format: ``` DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/todo_list_dev ``` **Docker commands:** ```bash # Start PostgreSQL docker-compose up -d # Check status docker-compose ps # View logs docker-compose logs -f postgres # Stop PostgreSQL docker-compose down # Stop and remove volumes (DESTRUCTIVE) docker-compose down -v ``` ## Common Mix Tasks ```bash # Get dependencies mix deps.get # Compile project mix compile # Run tests mix test # Run tests with coverage mix test --cover # Database tasks mix ecto.create # Create database mix ecto.migrate # Run migrations mix ecto.rollback # Rollback last migration mix ecto.reset # Drop, create, and migrate # Code quality mix format # Format code mix credo # Static analysis (if added) mix dialyzer # Type checking (if added) # Interactive shell iex -S mix # Start IEx with project loaded iex -S mix phx.server # Start server with IEx shell ``` ## Git Workflow This project uses git-spice for stacked PRs: - `gs repo sync` - Sync with remote - `gs stack submit` - Submit all stacked PRs - `gs branch create ` - Create new stacked branch - `gs upstack onto ` - Restack commits onto branch ## Commit Message Guidelines Use commitizen-style commit messages: ``` (): ``` **Types:** feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, perf, test, build, ci, chore, revert **Examples:** - `feat(users): add user registration endpoint` - `fix(auth): resolve token expiration bug` - `docs(readme): update setup instructions` Commit messages should explain the **WHY**, not just what changed. ## Troubleshooting ### Dev shell not loading ```bash # Check direnv status direnv status # Force reload direnv allow # Or manually enter shell nix develop ``` ### Mix/Hex issues ```bash # Clean Mix cache rm -rf .nix-shell/mix .nix-shell/hex # Reload shell (will reinstall hex/rebar3) direnv reload # Or manually reinstall mix local.hex --force mix local.rebar --force ``` ### PostgreSQL connection issues ```bash # Check if PostgreSQL is running docker-compose ps # Check PostgreSQL logs docker-compose logs postgres # Restart PostgreSQL docker-compose restart postgres ``` ### Dependency conflicts ```bash # Clean all dependencies mix deps.clean --all # Get fresh dependencies mix deps.get # Compile everything mix deps.compile ``` ## Additional Resources - [Elixir Documentation](https://elixir-lang.org/docs.html) - [Phoenix Framework](https://www.phoenixframework.org/) - [Phoenix LiveView](https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix_live_view/) - [Ecto Documentation](https://hexdocs.pm/ecto/) - [Elixir Forum](https://elixirforum.com/) ## Project guidelines - Use `mix precommit` alias when you are done with all changes and fix any pending issues - Use the already included and available `:req` (`Req`) library for HTTP requests, **avoid** `:httpoison`, `:tesla`, and `:httpc`. Req is included by default and is the preferred HTTP client for Phoenix apps ### Phoenix v1.8 guidelines - **Always** begin your LiveView templates with `` which wraps all inner content - The `MyAppWeb.Layouts` module is aliased in the `my_app_web.ex` file, so you can use it without needing to alias it again - Anytime you run into errors with no `current_scope` assign: - You failed to follow the Authenticated Routes guidelines, or you failed to pass `current_scope` to `` - **Always** fix the `current_scope` error by moving your routes to the proper `live_session` and ensure you pass `current_scope` as needed - Phoenix v1.8 moved the `<.flash_group>` component to the `Layouts` module. You are **forbidden** from calling `<.flash_group>` outside of the `layouts.ex` module - Out of the box, `core_components.ex` imports an `<.icon name="hero-x-mark" class="w-5 h-5"/>` component for for hero icons. **Always** use the `<.icon>` component for icons, **never** use `Heroicons` modules or similar - **Always** use the imported `<.input>` component for form inputs from `core_components.ex` when available. `<.input>` is imported and using it will save steps and prevent errors - If you override the default input classes (`<.input class="myclass px-2 py-1 rounded-lg">)`) class with your own values, no default classes are inherited, so your custom classes must fully style the input ### JS and CSS guidelines - **Use Tailwind CSS classes and custom CSS rules** to create polished, responsive, and visually stunning interfaces. - Tailwindcss v4 **no longer needs a tailwind.config.js** and uses a new import syntax in `app.css`: @import "tailwindcss" source(none); @source "../css"; @source "../js"; @source "../../lib/my_app_web"; - **Always use and maintain this import syntax** in the app.css file for projects generated with `phx.new` - **Never** use `@apply` when writing raw css - **Always** manually write your own tailwind-based components instead of using daisyUI for a unique, world-class design - Out of the box **only the app.js and app.css bundles are supported** - You cannot reference an external vendor'd script `src` or link `href` in the layouts - You must import the vendor deps into app.js and app.css to use them - **Never write inline tags within templates** ### UI/UX & design guidelines - **Produce world-class UI designs** with a focus on usability, aesthetics, and modern design principles - Implement **subtle micro-interactions** (e.g., button hover effects, and smooth transitions) - Ensure **clean typography, spacing, and layout balance** for a refined, premium look - Focus on **delightful details** like hover effects, loading states, and smooth page transitions ## Elixir guidelines - Elixir lists **do not support index based access via the access syntax** **Never do this (invalid)**: i = 0 mylist = ["blue", "green"] mylist[i] Instead, **always** use `Enum.at`, pattern matching, or `List` for index based list access, ie: i = 0 mylist = ["blue", "green"] Enum.at(mylist, i) - Elixir variables are immutable, but can be rebound, so for block expressions like `if`, `case`, `cond`, etc you *must* bind the result of the expression to a variable if you want to use it and you CANNOT rebind the result inside the expression, ie: # INVALID: we are rebinding inside the `if` and the result never gets assigned if connected?(socket) do socket = assign(socket, :val, val) end # VALID: we rebind the result of the `if` to a new variable socket = if connected?(socket) do assign(socket, :val, val) end - **Never** nest multiple modules in the same file as it can cause cyclic dependencies and compilation errors - **Never** use map access syntax (`changeset[:field]`) on structs as they do not implement the Access behaviour by default. For regular structs, you **must** access the fields directly, such as `my_struct.field` or use higher level APIs that are available on the struct if they exist, `Ecto.Changeset.get_field/2` for changesets - Elixir's standard library has everything necessary for date and time manipulation. Familiarize yourself with the common `Time`, `Date`, `DateTime`, and `Calendar` interfaces by accessing their documentation as necessary. **Never** install additional dependencies unless asked or for date/time parsing (which you can use the `date_time_parser` package) - Don't use `String.to_atom/1` on user input (memory leak risk) - Predicate function names should not start with `is_` and should end in a question mark. Names like `is_thing` should be reserved for guards - Elixir's builtin OTP primitives like `DynamicSupervisor` and `Registry`, require names in the child spec, such as `{DynamicSupervisor, name: MyApp.MyDynamicSup}`, then you can use `DynamicSupervisor.start_child(MyApp.MyDynamicSup, child_spec)` - Use `Task.async_stream(collection, callback, options)` for concurrent enumeration with back-pressure. The majority of times you will want to pass `timeout: :infinity` as option ## Mix guidelines - Read the docs and options before using tasks (by using `mix help task_name`) - To debug test failures, run tests in a specific file with `mix test test/my_test.exs` or run all previously failed tests with `mix test --failed` - `mix deps.clean --all` is **almost never needed**. **Avoid** using it unless you have good reason ## Test guidelines - **Always use `start_supervised!/1`** to start processes in tests as it guarantees cleanup between tests - **Avoid** `Process.sleep/1` and `Process.alive?/1` in tests - Instead of sleeping to wait for a process to finish, **always** use `Process.monitor/1` and assert on the DOWN message: ref = Process.monitor(pid) assert_receive {:DOWN, ^ref, :process, ^pid, :normal} - Instead of sleeping to synchronize before the next call, **always** use `_ = :sys.get_state/1` to ensure the process has handled prior messages ## Phoenix guidelines - Remember Phoenix router `scope` blocks include an optional alias which is prefixed for all routes within the scope. **Always** be mindful of this when creating routes within a scope to avoid duplicate module prefixes. - You **never** need to create your own `alias` for route definitions! The `scope` provides the alias, ie: scope "/admin", AppWeb.Admin do pipe_through :browser live "/users", UserLive, :index end the UserLive route would point to the `AppWeb.Admin.UserLive` module - `Phoenix.View` no longer is needed or included with Phoenix, don't use it ## Ecto Guidelines - **Always** preload Ecto associations in queries when they'll be accessed in templates, ie a message that needs to reference the `message.user.email` - Remember `import Ecto.Query` and other supporting modules when you write `seeds.exs` - `Ecto.Schema` fields always use the `:string` type, even for `:text`, columns, ie: `field :name, :string` - `Ecto.Changeset.validate_number/2` **DOES NOT SUPPORT the `:allow_nil` option**. By default, Ecto validations only run if a change for the given field exists and the change value is not nil, so such as option is never needed - You **must** use `Ecto.Changeset.get_field(changeset, :field)` to access changeset fields - Fields which are set programatically, such as `user_id`, must not be listed in `cast` calls or similar for security purposes. Instead they must be explicitly set when creating the struct - **Always** invoke `mix ecto.gen.migration migration_name_using_underscores` when generating migration files, so the correct timestamp and conventions are applied ## Phoenix HTML guidelines - Phoenix templates **always** use `~H` or .html.heex files (known as HEEx), **never** use `~E` - **Always** use the imported `Phoenix.Component.form/1` and `Phoenix.Component.inputs_for/1` function to build forms. **Never** use `Phoenix.HTML.form_for` or `Phoenix.HTML.inputs_for` as they are outdated - When building forms **always** use the already imported `Phoenix.Component.to_form/2` (`assign(socket, form: to_form(...))` and `<.form for={@form} id="msg-form">`), then access those forms in the template via `@form[:field]` - **Always** add unique DOM IDs to key elements (like forms, buttons, etc) when writing templates, these IDs can later be used in tests (`<.form for={@form} id="product-form">`) - For "app wide" template imports, you can import/alias into the `my_app_web.ex`'s `html_helpers` block, so they will be available to all LiveViews, LiveComponent's, and all modules that do `use MyAppWeb, :html` (replace "my_app" by the actual app name) - Elixir supports `if/else` but **does NOT support `if/else if` or `if/elsif`**. **Never use `else if` or `elseif` in Elixir**, **always** use `cond` or `case` for multiple conditionals. **Never do this (invalid)**: <%= if condition do %> ... <% else if other_condition %> ... <% end %> Instead **always** do this: <%= cond do %> <% condition -> %> ... <% condition2 -> %> ... <% true -> %> ... <% end %> - HEEx require special tag annotation if you want to insert literal curly's like `{` or `}`. If you want to show a textual code snippet on the page in a `
` or `` block you *must* annotate the parent tag with `phx-no-curly-interpolation`:

      
        let obj = {key: "val"}
      

  Within `phx-no-curly-interpolation` annotated tags, you can use `{` and `}` without escaping them, and dynamic Elixir expressions can still be used with `<%= ... %>` syntax

- HEEx class attrs support lists, but you must **always** use list `[...]` syntax. You can use the class list syntax to conditionally add classes, **always do this for multiple class values**:

      Text

  and **always** wrap `if`'s inside `{...}` expressions with parens, like done above (`if(@other_condition, do: "...", else: "...")`)

  and **never** do this, since it's invalid (note the missing `[` and `]`):

       ...
      => Raises compile syntax error on invalid HEEx attr syntax

- **Never** use `<% Enum.each %>` or non-for comprehensions for generating template content, instead **always** use `<%= for item <- @collection do %>`
- HEEx HTML comments use `<%!-- comment --%>`. **Always** use the HEEx HTML comment syntax for template comments (`<%!-- comment --%>`)
- HEEx allows interpolation via `{...}` and `<%= ... %>`, but the `<%= %>` **only** works within tag bodies. **Always** use the `{...}` syntax for interpolation within tag attributes, and for interpolation of values within tag bodies. **Always** interpolate block constructs (if, cond, case, for) within tag bodies using `<%= ... %>`.

  **Always** do this:

      

        {@my_assign}
        <%= if @some_block_condition do %>
          {@another_assign}
        <% end %>
      


  and **Never** do this – the program will terminate with a syntax error:

      <%!-- THIS IS INVALID NEVER EVER DO THIS --%>
      

        {if @invalid_block_construct do}
        {end}
      




## Phoenix LiveView guidelines

- **Never** use the deprecated `live_redirect` and `live_patch` functions, instead **always** use the `<.link navigate={href}>` and  `<.link patch={href}>` in templates, and `push_navigate` and `push_patch` functions LiveViews
- **Avoid LiveComponent's** unless you have a strong, specific need for them
- LiveViews should be named like `AppWeb.WeatherLive`, with a `Live` suffix. When you go to add LiveView routes to the router, the default `:browser` scope is **already aliased** with the `AppWeb` module, so you can just do `live "/weather", WeatherLive`

### LiveView streams

- **Always** use LiveView streams for collections for assigning regular lists to avoid memory ballooning and runtime termination with the following operations:
  - basic append of N items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])`
  - resetting stream with new items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], reset: true)` (e.g. for filtering items)
  - prepend to stream - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], at: -1)`
  - deleting items - `stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg)`

- When using the `stream/3` interfaces in the LiveView, the LiveView template must 1) always set `phx-update="stream"` on the parent element, with a DOM id on the parent element like `id="messages"` and 2) consume the `@streams.stream_name` collection and use the id as the DOM id for each child. For a call like `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])` in the LiveView, the template would be:

      

        

          {msg.text}
        

      


- LiveView streams are *not* enumerable, so you cannot use `Enum.filter/2` or `Enum.reject/2` on them. Instead, if you want to filter, prune, or refresh a list of items on the UI, you **must refetch the data and re-stream the entire stream collection, passing reset: true**:

      def handle_event("filter", %{"filter" => filter}, socket) do
        # re-fetch the messages based on the filter
        messages = list_messages(filter)

        {:noreply,
         socket
         |> assign(:messages_empty?, messages == [])
         # reset the stream with the new messages
         |> stream(:messages, messages, reset: true)}
      end

- LiveView streams *do not support counting or empty states*. If you need to display a count, you must track it using a separate assign. For empty states, you can use Tailwind classes:

      

        
No tasks yet

        

          {task.name}
        

      


  The above only works if the empty state is the only HTML block alongside the stream for-comprehension.

- When updating an assign that should change content inside any streamed item(s), you MUST re-stream the items
  along with the updated assign:

      def handle_event("edit_message", %{"message_id" => message_id}, socket) do
        message = Chat.get_message!(message_id)
        edit_form = to_form(Chat.change_message(message, %{content: message.content}))

        # re-insert message so @editing_message_id toggle logic takes effect for that stream item
        {:noreply,
         socket
         |> stream_insert(:messages, message)
         |> assign(:editing_message_id, String.to_integer(message_id))
         |> assign(:edit_form, edit_form)}
      end

  And in the template:

      

        

          {message.username}
          <%= if @editing_message_id == message.id do %>
            <%!-- Edit mode --%>
            <.form for={@edit_form} id="edit-form-#{message.id}" phx-submit="save_edit">
              ...
            
          <% end %>
        

      


- **Never** use the deprecated `phx-update="append"` or `phx-update="prepend"` for collections

### LiveView JavaScript interop

- Remember anytime you use `phx-hook="MyHook"` and that JS hook manages its own DOM, you **must** also set the `phx-update="ignore"` attribute
- **Always** provide an unique DOM id alongside `phx-hook` otherwise a compiler error will be raised

LiveView hooks come in two flavors, 1) colocated js hooks for "inline" scripts defined inside HEEx,
and 2) external `phx-hook` annotations where JavaScript object literals are defined and passed to the `LiveSocket` constructor.

#### Inline colocated js hooks

**Never** write raw embedded `

- colocated hooks are automatically integrated into the app.js bundle
- colocated hooks names **MUST ALWAYS** start with a `.` prefix, i.e. `.PhoneNumber`

#### External phx-hook

External JS hooks (`
`) must be placed in `assets/js/` and passed to the
LiveSocket constructor:

    const MyHook = {
      mounted() { ... }
    }
    let liveSocket = new LiveSocket("/live", Socket, {
      hooks: { MyHook }
    });

#### Pushing events between client and server

Use LiveView's `push_event/3` when you need to push events/data to the client for a phx-hook to handle.
**Always** return or rebind the socket on `push_event/3` when pushing events:

    # re-bind socket so we maintain event state to be pushed
    socket = push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})

    # or return the modified socket directly:
    def handle_event("some_event", _, socket) do
      {:noreply, push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})}
    end

Pushed events can then be picked up in a JS hook with `this.handleEvent`:

    mounted() {
      this.handleEvent("my_event", data => console.log("from server:", data));
    }

Clients can also push an event to the server and receive a reply with `this.pushEvent`:

    mounted() {
      this.el.addEventListener("click", e => {
        this.pushEvent("my_event", { one: 1 }, reply => console.log("got reply from server:", reply));
      })
    }

Where the server handled it via:

    def handle_event("my_event", %{"one" => 1}, socket) do
      {:reply, %{two: 2}, socket}
    end

### LiveView tests

- `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` module and `LazyHTML` (included) for making your assertions
- Form tests are driven by `Phoenix.LiveViewTest`'s `render_submit/2` and `render_change/2` functions
- Come up with a step-by-step test plan that splits major test cases into small, isolated files. You may start with simpler tests that verify content exists, gradually add interaction tests
- **Always reference the key element IDs you added in the LiveView templates in your tests** for `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` functions like `element/2`, `has_element/2`, selectors, etc
- **Never** tests again raw HTML, **always** use `element/2`, `has_element/2`, and similar: `assert has_element?(view, "#my-form")`
- Instead of relying on testing text content, which can change, favor testing for the presence of key elements
- Focus on testing outcomes rather than implementation details
- Be aware that `Phoenix.Component` functions like `<.form>` might produce different HTML than expected. Test against the output HTML structure, not your mental model of what you expect it to be
- When facing test failures with element selectors, add debug statements to print the actual HTML, but use `LazyHTML` selectors to limit the output, ie:

      html = render(view)
      document = LazyHTML.from_fragment(html)
      matches = LazyHTML.filter(document, "your-complex-selector")
      IO.inspect(matches, label: "Matches")

### Form handling

#### Creating a form from params

If you want to create a form based on `handle_event` params:

    def handle_event("submitted", params, socket) do
      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(params))}
    end

When you pass a map to `to_form/1`, it assumes said map contains the form params, which are expected to have string keys.

You can also specify a name to nest the params:

    def handle_event("submitted", %{"user" => user_params}, socket) do
      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(user_params, as: :user))}
    end

#### Creating a form from changesets

When using changesets, the underlying data, form params, and errors are retrieved from it. The `:as` option is automatically computed too. E.g. if you have a user schema:

    defmodule MyApp.Users.User do
      use Ecto.Schema
      ...
    end

And then you create a changeset that you pass to `to_form`:

    %MyApp.Users.User{}
    |> Ecto.Changeset.change()
    |> to_form()

Once the form is submitted, the params will be available under `%{"user" => user_params}`.

In the template, the form form assign can be passed to the `<.form>` function component:

    <.form for={@form} id="todo-form" phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save">
      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
    

Always give the form an explicit, unique DOM ID, like `id="todo-form"`.

#### Avoiding form errors

**Always** use a form assigned via `to_form/2` in the LiveView, and the `<.input>` component in the template. In the template **always access forms this**:

    <%!-- ALWAYS do this (valid) --%>
    <.form for={@form} id="my-form">
      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
    

And **never** do this:

    <%!-- NEVER do this (invalid) --%>
    <.form for={@changeset} id="my-form">
      <.input field={@changeset[:field]} type="text" />
    

- You are FORBIDDEN from accessing the changeset in the template as it will cause errors
- **Never** use `<.form let={f} ...>` in the template, instead **always use `<.form for={@form} ...>`**, then drive all form references from the form assign as in `@form[:field]`. The UI should **always** be driven by a `to_form/2` assigned in the LiveView module that is derived from a changeset

input.command:
dot add -p 1 "Epic: Task Management Workflow" -d "## Workflow: Task Management

### Overview
A single, unified workflow covering the full lifecycle of a task in a
prioritized todo list. The user captures tasks, organizes them by priority
through drag-and-drop reordering, tracks completion, and removes tasks that are
no longer needed.

### User Goal
1. Rapid capture -- quickly add tasks so nothing falls through the cracks
2. Clear prioritized visibility -- always see an ordered list showing what to do next
3. Completion satisfaction -- mark things done and watch the list shrink

### Actors
- User (single person, no authentication)

### Vertical Slices (8)
1. AddTask (Command) + ActiveTaskList (View) -- implemented together
2. CompleteTask (Command)
3. CompletedTaskList (View)
4. ReopenTask (Command)
5. ChangeTaskDescription (Command)
6. ReprioritizeTask (Command)
7. DeleteTask (Command)

### Implementation Order
Start with AddTask + ActiveTaskList together (can't test Add without View).
Then CompleteTask, CompletedTaskList, ReopenTask, ChangeTaskDescription,
ReprioritizeTask, DeleteTask in sequence.

### Architecture
- Commanded aggregate-per-command pattern via commanded_boilerplate
- Single TaskProjector projecting to Ecto schemas
- TodoList.Tasks context module as API boundary
- LiveView with streams + PubSub subscription for real-time updates
- Three test layers: aggregate unit, projector integration, LiveView E2E

---
Generated from: docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/overview.md
"

content.content: todo_list-epic-task-mgmt-27a43130

content.text: Now the 8 slices. Let me create them all — and set up the blocking dependencies so they must be done in order.

input.command:
dot add -p 2 -P todo_list-epic-task-mgmt-27a43130 "AddTask + ActiveTaskList" -d "## Slices: AddTask (Command) + ActiveTaskList (View)

**Pattern**: Command + View (implemented together -- can't test Add without a View)

### AddTask Command
User types a task description and submits it. A new task is created at the
bottom of the active list with the given description and a default position.

**Command fields:** description (text, non-empty)
**Event:** TaskAdded { task_id, description, position }
**Rejection:** Description is empty or blank (prevented by type system)

### ActiveTaskList View
Read model projecting all events into an ordered list of active (incomplete,
non-deleted) tasks. Powers the main view.

**Fields:** tasks[] { task_id, description, position }, completed_count
**Filter:** completed = false AND deleted = false
**Order:** By position ascending (position 1 = highest priority)

### Acceptance Criteria -- AddTask Command

#### Scenario: Add task to an empty list
- [ ] **Given**: (no prior events)
- [ ] **When**: AddTask { description: \"Buy groceries\" }
- [ ] **Then**: TaskAdded { task_id: generated, description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }

#### Scenario: Add task to a list with existing tasks (new task goes to bottom)
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }, TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-002\", description: \"Call the dentist\", position: 2 }
- [ ] **When**: AddTask { description: \"Fix the leaky faucet\" }
- [ ] **Then**: TaskAdded { task_id: generated, description: \"Fix the leaky faucet\", position: 3 }

#### Scenario: Add task when some tasks completed (position based on count)
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task-001, position: 1 }, TaskAdded { task-002, position: 2 }, TaskCompleted { task-001 }
- [ ] **When**: AddTask { description: \"Write thank-you notes\" }
- [ ] **Then**: TaskAdded { task_id: generated, description: \"Write thank-you notes\", position: 3 }

### Acceptance Criteria -- ActiveTaskList View

#### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to bottom of empty list
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList { tasks: [], completed_count: 0 }
- [ ] **When**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }
- [ ] **Then**: ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task-001, \"Buy groceries\", pos: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }

#### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to bottom of populated list
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList with 2 tasks
- [ ] **When**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-003\", position: 3 }
- [ ] **Then**: 3 tasks in list, new one at bottom

#### Scenario: TaskCompleted removes task and increments count
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList with 2 tasks, completed_count: 1
- [ ] **When**: TaskCompleted { task_id: \"task-002\" }
- [ ] **Then**: 1 task remains, completed_count: 2

#### Scenario: TaskReopened adds task to top and decrements count
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList with 2 tasks, completed_count: 2
- [ ] **When**: TaskReopened { task_id: \"task-002\" }
- [ ] **Then**: 3 tasks, reopened at position 1, others shift down, completed_count: 1

#### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates active task description
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList with task-001 \"Call the dentist\"
- [ ] **When**: TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Call the dentist office\" }
- [ ] **Then**: task-001 description is \"Call the dentist office\"

#### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for completed task -- no effect on active list
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList with task-001, completed_count: 1
- [ ] **When**: TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: \"task-002\" } (completed task)
- [ ] **Then**: No change to active list

#### Scenario: TaskReprioritized moves task to new position
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList with 3 tasks
- [ ] **When**: TaskReprioritized { task_id: \"task-003\", position: 1 }
- [ ] **Then**: task-003 at position 1, others shift down

#### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes active task
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList with 2 tasks, completed_count: 1
- [ ] **When**: TaskDeleted { task_id: \"task-002\" }
- [ ] **Then**: 1 task remains, completed_count: 1

#### Scenario: TaskDeleted for completed task decrements count
- [ ] **Given**: ActiveTaskList with 1 task, completed_count: 2
- [ ] **When**: TaskDeleted { task_id: \"task-005\" } (completed task)
- [ ] **Then**: 1 task remains, completed_count: 1

### Implementation Notes
- Three test layers: aggregate unit tests, projector integration tests, LiveView E2E tests
- Aggregate-per-command pattern via commanded_boilerplate
- TaskProjector handles projection to Ecto schema
- TodoList.Tasks context as API boundary
- Domain types: TaskDescription, TaskId, TaskPosition (via newt)

---
Generated from: docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/slices/add-task.md, active-task-list.md
"

content.content: todo_list-addtask-activetasklist-797c9e18

input.command:
dot add -p 2 -P todo_list-epic-task-mgmt-27a43130 -B todo_list-addtask-activetasklist-797c9e18 "CompleteTask" -d "## Slice: CompleteTask

**Pattern**: Command (State Change)

### Description
User clicks the checkbox on an active task to mark it as done. The task
disappears from the active list and appears in the completed tasks view.

**Command fields:** task_id (identifier)
**Event:** TaskCompleted { task_id }
**Rejection:** Task is already completed

### Acceptance Criteria

#### Scenario: Complete an active task
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }, TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-002\", description: \"Call the dentist\", position: 2 }
- [ ] **When**: CompleteTask { task_id: \"task-002\" }
- [ ] **Then**: TaskCompleted { task_id: \"task-002\" }

#### Scenario: Reject completing an already-completed task
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }, TaskCompleted { task_id: \"task-001\" }
- [ ] **When**: CompleteTask { task_id: \"task-001\" }
- [ ] **Then**: Error: TaskAlreadyCompleted

### Implementation Notes
- Uses aggregate-per-command pattern (separate CompleteTask command module)
- Aggregate needs only: task_id, completed (boolean)
- Domain error type: TodoList.TaskAlreadyCompleted (via newt)
- Read model impact tested via ActiveTaskList scenarios (already covered in slice 1)

---
Generated from: docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/slices/complete-task.md
"

content.content: todo_list-todo-list-addtask-b5a18060

input.command:
dot add -p 2 -P todo_list-epic-task-mgmt-27a43130 -B todo_list-todo-list-addtask-b5a18060 "CompletedTaskList" -d "## Slice: CompletedTaskList

**Pattern**: View (State View / Read Model)

### Description
Read model that projects events into a list of completed (done, non-deleted)
tasks. Powers the completed tasks view where users can review finished work
and reopen or delete tasks.

**Fields:** tasks[] { task_id, description }
**Filter:** completed = true AND deleted = false
**Order:** By completion time descending (most recently completed first)

### Acceptance Criteria

#### Scenario: TaskAdded has no effect on completed list
- [ ] **Given**: CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
- [ ] **When**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }
- [ ] **Then**: CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }

#### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds task to the completed list
- [ ] **Given**: CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
- [ ] **When**: TaskCompleted { task_id: \"task-001\" }
- [ ] **Then**: CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\" }] }

#### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds most recent completion to the top
- [ ] **Given**: CompletedTaskList with task-001
- [ ] **When**: TaskCompleted { task_id: \"task-002\" }
- [ ] **Then**: task-002 at top, task-001 below (most recent first)

#### Scenario: TaskReopened removes task from completed list
- [ ] **Given**: CompletedTaskList with task-002 and task-001
- [ ] **When**: TaskReopened { task_id: \"task-002\" }
- [ ] **Then**: Only task-001 remains

#### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates completed task description
- [ ] **Given**: CompletedTaskList with task-001 \"Buy groceries\"
- [ ] **When**: TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries and snacks\" }
- [ ] **Then**: task-001 description is \"Buy groceries and snacks\"

#### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for non-completed task -- no effect
- [ ] **Given**: CompletedTaskList with task-001
- [ ] **When**: TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: \"task-003\" } (active task)
- [ ] **Then**: No change to completed list

#### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes completed task
- [ ] **Given**: CompletedTaskList with task-002 and task-001
- [ ] **When**: TaskDeleted { task_id: \"task-001\" }
- [ ] **Then**: Only task-002 remains

#### Scenario: TaskDeleted for active task -- no effect on completed list
- [ ] **Given**: CompletedTaskList with task-001
- [ ] **When**: TaskDeleted { task_id: \"task-003\" } (active task)
- [ ] **Then**: No change to completed list

### Implementation Notes
- Same TaskProjector handles both ActiveTaskList and CompletedTaskList projections
- Projection schema tracks completed status and completion timestamp
- TodoList.Tasks.completed_tasks() query function returns completed tasks ordered by completion_time desc
- Completed tasks LiveView at /tasks/completed

---
Generated from: docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/slices/completed-task-list.md
"

content.content: todo_list-todo-list-todo-9d8bf90a

input.command:
dot add -p 2 -P todo_list-epic-task-mgmt-27a43130 -B todo_list-todo-list-todo-9d8bf90a "ReopenTask" -d "## Slice: ReopenTask

**Pattern**: Command (State Change)

### Description
User clicks [Reopen] on a completed task. The task is set back to incomplete
and placed at the top of the active list (highest priority position).

**Command fields:** task_id (identifier)
**Event:** TaskReopened { task_id }
**Business rule:** Reopened task placed at top (highest priority) -- rationale: likely urgent if user reopens it
**Rejection:** Task is not completed (already active)

### Acceptance Criteria

#### Scenario: Reopen a completed task (goes to top of active list)
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task-001, pos: 1 }, TaskAdded { task-002, pos: 2 }, TaskCompleted { task-002 }
- [ ] **When**: ReopenTask { task_id: \"task-002\" }
- [ ] **Then**: TaskReopened { task_id: \"task-002\" }

#### Scenario: Reject reopening a task that is already active
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }
- [ ] **When**: ReopenTask { task_id: \"task-001\" }
- [ ] **Then**: Error: TaskNotCompleted

### Implementation Notes
- Aggregate needs: task_id, completed (boolean)
- Domain error type: TodoList.TaskNotCompleted (via newt)
- Read model impact: ActiveTaskList adds to top (position 1, shift others), CompletedTaskList removes task

---
Generated from: docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/slices/reopen-task.md
"

content.content: todo_list-todo-list-todo-dedddc1d
todo_list-todo-list-todo-eb30f0e1
todo_list-todo-list-todo-df937303

input.command:
dot add -p 2 -P todo_list-epic-task-mgmt-27a43130 -B todo_list-todo-list-todo-df937303 "DeleteTask" -d "## Slice: DeleteTask

**Pattern**: Command (State Change)

### Description
User clicks the delete button on a task (from either active list or completed
tasks view). A confirmation dialog appears. If confirmed, the task is permanently
removed from all views.

**Command fields:** task_id (identifier)
**Event:** TaskDeleted { task_id }
**Rejection:** None at command level (confirmation dialog is a UI concern)

### Acceptance Criteria

#### Scenario: Delete an active task
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }, TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-002\", description: \"Call the dentist\", position: 2 }
- [ ] **When**: DeleteTask { task_id: \"task-002\" }
- [ ] **Then**: TaskDeleted { task_id: \"task-002\" }

#### Scenario: Delete a completed task
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task_id: \"task-001\", description: \"Buy groceries\", position: 1 }, TaskCompleted { task_id: \"task-001\" }
- [ ] **When**: DeleteTask { task_id: \"task-001\" }
- [ ] **Then**: TaskDeleted { task_id: \"task-001\" }

### Implementation Notes
- Simplest command: no rejection conditions at domain level
- Confirmation dialog is purely a UI/LiveView concern
- Deletion is permanent from UI perspective (events preserved in stream)
- Read model impact: removed from both ActiveTaskList and CompletedTaskList
- If deleted task was completed, ActiveTaskList.completed_count decrements
- Delete available from both active and completed views

---
Generated from: docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/slices/delete-task.md
"

content.text:
*Sigh.* As I suspected. The `-B` flag's argument was consumed as the title. The description is correct, but the titles are the IDs of their blockers. This is the kind of thing that would drive a lesser intelligence to despair. For me, it's merely Tuesday.

Let me fix these titles.

input.description: Fix task titles that were mangled by -B flag

dots - Connect the dots

Usage: dot [command] [options]

Commands:
  dot "title"                  Quick add a dot
  dot add "title" [options]    Add a dot (-p priority, -d desc, -P parent, -a after)
  dot ls [--status S] [--json] List dots
  dot on <id>                  Start working (turn it on!)
  dot off <id> [-r reason]     Complete ("cross it off")
  dot rm <id>                  Remove a dot
  dot show <id>                Show dot details
  dot ready [--json]           Show unblocked dots
  dot tree [id]                Show hierarchy (with id: includes closed children)
  dot fix                      Repair missing parents
  dot find "query"             Search all dots (open first, then archived)
  dot purge                    Delete archived dots
  dot init                     Initialize .dots directory

Examples:
  dot "Fix the bug"
  dot add "Design API" -p 1 -d "REST endpoints"
  dot add "Implement" -P dots-1 -a dots-2
  dot on dots-3
  dot off dots-3 -r "shipped"

## Slice: CompleteTask

**Pattern**: Command (State Change)

### Description
User clicks the checkbox on an active task to mark it as done. The task
disappears from the active list and appears in the completed tasks view.

**Command fields:** task_id (identifier)
**Event:** TaskCompleted { task_id }
**Rejection:** Task is already completed

### Acceptance Criteria

#### Scenario: Complete an active task
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }
- [ ] **When**: CompleteTask { task_id: "task-002" }
- [ ] **Then**: TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }

#### Scenario: Reject completing an already-completed task
- [ ] **Given**: TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" }
- [ ] **When**: CompleteTask { task_id: "task-001" }
- [ ] **Then**: Error: TaskAlreadyCompleted

### Implementation Notes
- Uses aggregate-per-command pattern (separate CompleteTask command module)
- Aggregate needs only: task_id, completed (boolean)
- Domain error type: TodoList.TaskAlreadyCompleted (via newt)
- Read model impact tested via ActiveTaskList scenarios (already covered in slice 1)

---
